issues.title: Virginia Congressional Redistricting Amendment — PASSED, NOW BLOCKED IN COURT
issues.summary: Statewide constitutional amendment (HJ6007) PASSED on April 21, 2026 with 51.59% Yes / 48.41% No. A judge issued an injunction the next day blocking certification; AG Jay Jones is appealing. Authorizes the General Assembly to temporarily redraw U.S. congressional district maps, overriding the bipartisan commission voters approved in 2020.
issues.body:
UPDATE — April 25, 2026: The amendment passed at the polls on April 21 with 51.59% Yes (1,588,298) to 48.41% No (1,490,564) — a margin of just under 100,000 votes out of 3.08 million ballots cast. The next day, April 22, a Virginia circuit-court judge issued an injunction blocking certification of the result. Attorney General Jay Jones (D) has announced his intent to appeal. As of this writing the amendment is approved by voters but its implementation is on hold pending appellate review.

The Vote

  • YES: 1,588,298 (51.59%)
  • NO: 1,490,564 (48.41%)
  • Total ballots cast: 3,078,862
  • Margin: ~97,734 votes (3.18 percentage points)

What The Amendment Does (If Certified)

  • Strips the bipartisan commission: In 2020, Virginia voters approved a bipartisan redistricting commission. This amendment suspends that commission temporarily and hands map-drawing power back to the Democrat-controlled General Assembly until after the 2030 census.
  • Current map: Republicans hold 5 seats, Democrats hold 6 seats (11 total).
  • Proposed new map (favored by supporters): Democrats would hold 10 of 11 seats — a 4-seat shift.
  • Funding disparity at the polls: "Virginians for Fair Elections" (YES) raised $49 million. "Virginians for Fair Maps" (NO) raised $9 million.

Pre-vote Polling vs. Result

Washington Post / Schar School poll (March 26-31, 2026): Support 52%, Oppose 47%. The actual result tracked the polling closely (51.59% / 48.41%).

Litigation Status

The April 22 injunction halts certification of the vote pending the appeal. Even if the appellate court ultimately upholds the result, the new congressional map cannot be enacted until certification is permitted to proceed. RESOLUTE Citizen will update this issue as the court schedule develops.
issues.analysis: This amendment was a power grab dressed in the language of fairness, and the voters of Virginia narrowly approved it. Virginia voters in 2020 had chosen to take redistricting out of partisan hands; this amendment reversed that decision and concentrates map-drawing power in a Democrat-controlled legislature that has voted to expand abortion, ban firearms, promote gender ideology in schools, and suppress parental rights. Christians who value the election of godly magistrates should understand what the YES result means if certification holds: fewer representatives who share their values in Congress, harder paths to victory for any Republican challenger, and a map drawn for the next four cycles by people who voted against every RESOLUTE Citizen criterion. The court injunction is the only remaining check; pray for righteous judges and engaged citizens. The narrow margin — under 3.2 points — is a reminder that local turnout matters; the next ballot question (and there will be one) is winnable by either side.
issues.scripture: The king's heart is in the hand of the LORD, as the rivers of water: he turneth it whithersoever he will.
supporters: U.S. Senators Tim Kaine and Mark Warner
opponents: Former Governor Glenn Youngkin (R)

issues.title: 2025 Virginia Statewide Elections
issues.summary: Virginia held gubernatorial elections in 2025. Democrats swept all three statewide offices and retained the House of Delegates.
issues.body:
Results

  • Governor: Abigail Spanberger (D) defeated Winsome Earle-Sears (R)
  • Lieutenant Governor: Ghazala Hashmi (D) defeated John Reid (R)
  • Attorney General: Jay Jones (D) defeated Jason Miyares (R, incumbent)
  • House of Delegates: Democrats retained majority (64-36)

Impact on Christian Voters

The Democrat sweep means expanded abortion access, continued gun control measures, and progressive education policies for the next 4 years. The Governor controls judicial appointments and enforcement priorities. The AG sets legal policy on religious liberty cases.
issues.analysis: Virginia's 2025 election was a significant setback for conservative Christian values at the state level. All three statewide offices are now held by officials who oppose the scorecard's criteria. The House remains 64-36 Democrat. Christians must now focus on local races, school board elections, and 2027 state senate races as the path to restoration.
issues.scripture: If my people, which are called by my name, shall humble themselves, and pray, and seek my face, and turn from their wicked ways; then will I hear from heaven, and will forgive their sin, and will heal their land.

issues.title: School Board Watch: Spotsylvania, Stafford & Fredericksburg
issues.location: Spotsylvania County, Stafford County & City of Fredericksburg
issues.summary: School boards control curriculum, library materials, bathroom policies, and the moral environment your children spend 7+ hours in daily.
issues.body:
Key Issues

  • Sexually Explicit Materials: Spotsylvania made national news in 2021 when board members called for removing graphic books from school libraries. JLARC found Spotsylvania ranked 5th statewide for book removals.
  • Gender Ideology: Are schools using preferred pronouns, allowing opposite-sex bathroom access, or teaching gender theory?
  • Parental Notification: Are parents informed when their child expresses gender confusion at school?
  • Critical Race Theory: Stafford BOS passed anti-CRT/pronouns funding resolution. Is CRT or DEI ideology embedded in curriculum?
issues.analysis: Parents, not the state, are the primary educators of their children under God's design (Deuteronomy 6:6-7). School boards that undermine parental authority or expose children to sexually explicit or ideologically corrupt material are acting contrary to biblical principles. Attend school board meetings. Know your board members. Hold them accountable.
issues.scripture: And these words, which I command thee this day, shall be in thine heart: And thou shalt teach them diligently unto thy children.

issues.title: Texas Border Security & Operation Lone Star
issues.summary: Texas continues Operation Lone Star, the largest state-led border security operation in U.S. history. Governor Abbott has deployed National Guard troops and installed razor wire along the border.
issues.body:
Background

Operation Lone Star launched in March 2021 under Governor Greg Abbott to combat the surge of illegal immigration and drug trafficking at the southern border. Texas has spent over $11 billion on the operation.

Key Actions

  • National Guard deployment along the Rio Grande
  • Razor wire installation along border sections
  • Busing migrants to sanctuary cities (DC, New York, Chicago, Denver)
  • SB4 — state law allowing Texas law enforcement to arrest illegal border crossers
  • Buoy barrier in the Rio Grande (legal challenges ongoing)
issues.analysis: Scripture teaches that nations have borders and that lawful authority exists to protect citizens. Texas is acting where the federal government has abdicated its duty. Christians should support magistrates who enforce the law and protect their communities from the consequences of illegal immigration — fentanyl, human trafficking, and the strain on public resources.
issues.scripture: Let every soul be subject unto the higher powers. For there is no power but of God: the powers that be are ordained of God.

issues.title: Texas School Choice / Education Savings Accounts
issues.summary: Governor Abbott has made school choice his top legislative priority, pushing for Education Savings Accounts (ESAs) that would allow parents to use public funds for private or homeschool education.
issues.body:
What's at Stake

Texas is one of the last large conservative states without a school choice program. Rural Republicans have historically opposed ESAs, fearing funding loss to rural public schools. Governor Abbott successfully primaried several anti-choice Republicans in 2024.

Key Points

  • ESAs would give families ~$10,000/year per child for private school, homeschool, or tutoring
  • Rural vs suburban divide within the Republican party
  • Lt. Gov. Dan Patrick is a strong supporter
  • Teacher unions and Democrats oppose
issues.analysis: Parental authority over education is a core biblical principle. The ability to choose how and where your children are educated — including Christian schools and homeschool — is under the jurisdiction of the family, not the state. ESAs empower parents to make these decisions with their own tax dollars.
issues.scripture: Train up a child in the way he should go: and when he is old, he will not depart from it.

issues.title: Federal Second Amendment Developments
issues.summary: Tracking federal legislation and court decisions affecting the Second Amendment right to keep and bear arms.
issues.body:
Key Developments

  • SCOTUS — Bruen (2022): Landmark ruling establishing text-history-and-tradition test for gun regulations. Struck down NY concealed carry restrictions.
  • SCOTUS — Rahimi (2024): Upheld domestic violence restraining order gun prohibitions, narrowing Bruen somewhat.
  • State-level bans: Multiple states passed assault weapons bans and red flag laws post-Bruen. Virginia's SB2 assault weapons ban passed Senate 21-19 but was vetoed by Gov. Youngkin (2024).
issues.analysis: The right to armed self-defense is a God-given natural right and biblical duty, consistent with Reformed covenant theology and America's founding principles. Christians should oppose all efforts to disarm law-abiding citizens and support magistrates who defend the Second Amendment.
issues.scripture: He that hath no sword, let him sell his garment, and buy one.

sources.label: SCOTUS — New York State Rifle v. Bruen

issues.title: State Abortion Amendments & Pro-Life Movement Post-Dobbs
issues.summary: Since Dobbs v. Jackson (2022) overturned Roe v. Wade, abortion policy has returned to the states. Multiple states have passed constitutional amendments in both directions.
issues.body:
Landscape

  • Pro-life wins: 14 states have near-total bans in effect. Texas heartbeat law (SB8) and total ban post-Dobbs.
  • Pro-abortion amendments: Ohio, Michigan, California, Vermont, Kansas (failed), and others have passed state constitutional amendments codifying abortion access.
  • Virginia: SJR247 passed both chambers (2025-2026) — abortion rights constitutional amendment heading to voters.
issues.analysis: The unborn child bears God's image and possesses full personhood under biblical law. The state has a duty to protect innocent life. Christians must engage at the state level where this battle is now being fought — supporting pro-life candidates, opposing abortion amendments, and advocating for the total abolition of abortion.
issues.scripture: Before I formed thee in the belly I knew thee; and before thou camest forth out of the womb I sanctified thee.

sources.label: Americans United for Life — State Scorecard

issues.title: Missouri Amendment 3: Repeal Abortion Rights
issues.summary: Legislatively referred amendment to repeal the 2024 abortion rights amendment. Would prohibit abortion except in cases of medical emergency, fetal anomaly, rape (before 12 weeks), or incest (before 12 weeks). First time voters will decide to repeal a right-to-abortion amendment.
issues.body:
Missouri Amendment 3 is a legislatively referred constitutional amendment that would repeal the 2024 voter-approved Amendment 3, which established a fundamental right to reproductive freedom including abortion. The new measure would prohibit abortion except in cases of medical emergency, fetal anomaly, or rape/incest before 12 weeks.

This is a historic measure -- the first time voters anywhere in the nation will decide whether to repeal a previously approved right-to-abortion amendment. A Cole County judge approved the ballot language after forcing revisions to clarify the measure would 'repeal' rather than merely 'amend' existing protections.

The measure also includes a prohibition on gender transition procedures for minors.
issues.analysis: This is the most significant pro-life ballot measure in 2026. Christians who affirm the sanctity of life from conception should support this measure to restore protections for the unborn in Missouri.
issues.scripture: Before I formed thee in the belly I knew thee; and before thou camest forth out of the womb I sanctified thee.

issues.title: Missouri Amendment 4: Supermajority for Citizen Initiatives
issues.summary: Would require citizen initiatives to win majorities in all eight congressional districts to pass, not just a statewide majority. No citizen amendment since 2020 would have met this standard. Aims to prevent future progressive ballot measures.
issues.body:
Missouri Amendment 4 would fundamentally change the citizen initiative process by requiring ballot measures to win majorities in all eight of the state's congressional districts, rather than just a statewide simple majority. Analysts note that no citizen-initiated amendment since 2020 would have passed under this standard.

Supporters argue this prevents urban areas from imposing their will on rural communities. Opponents say it effectively ends direct democracy in Missouri. This measure is widely seen as a response to the 2024 abortion amendment passing via citizen initiative.
issues.analysis: While protecting conservative values from progressive ballot measures is the intent, Christians should weigh whether restricting the people's right to petition aligns with principles of just governance and self-determination.
issues.scripture: Where no counsel is, the people fall: but in the multitude of counsellors there is safety.

sources.label: Missouri Secretary of State - 2026 Ballot Measures

issues.title: Nevada Question 6: Abortion Rights (Second Vote)
issues.summary: Constitutional amendment establishing a fundamental right to abortion until fetal viability. Passed 64-36% in 2024 and must pass again in 2026 due to Nevada's two-election requirement for constitutional amendments.
issues.body:
Nevada Question 6 would establish a fundamental right to abortion in the state constitution, prohibiting government interference with abortion performed by a qualified healthcare professional until fetal viability, or when necessary to protect the life or health of the pregnant individual.

Because Nevada requires constitutional amendments to pass in two consecutive even-year elections, this measure returns to the ballot after passing 64% to 36% in 2024. If it passes again, the amendment becomes permanent constitutional law.

Pro-life advocates have one more chance to defeat this measure before it is permanently enshrined.
issues.analysis: This is the last opportunity to prevent abortion rights from being permanently enshrined in Nevada's constitution. Christians who value the sanctity of unborn life should be aware this requires a 'No' vote.
issues.scripture: For thou hast possessed my reins: thou hast covered me in my mother's womb.

issues.title: Nevada Question 7: Voter ID Requirement (Second Vote)
issues.summary: Constitutional amendment requiring photo ID for in-person voting and last four digits of SSN or driver's license for mail-in voting. Passed 53-47% in 2024; must pass again in 2026 to take effect.
issues.body:
Nevada Question 7 would amend the state constitution to require government-issued photo identification for in-person voting and the last four digits of a Social Security number or driver's license number for mail-in voting.

Like Question 6, this measure passed in 2024 (53% to 47%) and must pass again in 2026 due to Nevada's two-election constitutional amendment process. If approved again, it becomes permanent constitutional law.

Supporters say this is essential for election integrity. Opponents argue existing safeguards are sufficient and that ID requirements burden vulnerable voters.
issues.analysis: Election integrity is a matter of just governance. Requiring identification to vote ensures that the voice of lawful citizens is not diluted, consistent with biblical principles of honest weights and measures.
issues.scripture: A false balance is abomination to the LORD: but a just weight is his delight.

issues.title: Virginia: Right to Reproductive Freedom Amendment
issues.summary: Constitutional amendment guaranteeing the right to make decisions about pregnancy, including abortion. Placed on the ballot by the Democrat-controlled General Assembly. A lawsuit is pending to block its placement on the ballot.
issues.body:
The Virginia Right to Reproductive Freedom Amendment would amend the state constitution to establish 'the right to make and effectuate one's own decisions about all matters related to one's pregnancy.' The amendment was passed twice by the Virginia General Assembly (2025 and 2026 sessions) as required for referred amendments.

On March 3, 2026, Charla Bansely filed a lawsuit seeking to block the measure, alleging state election officials failed to distribute the amendment to circuit clerks in all counties as required by law.

This is one of four constitutional amendments placed on the November 2026 ballot by the Democrat-controlled legislature.
issues.analysis: Virginia Christians face a critical fight to prevent abortion from being permanently enshrined in the state constitution. This measure would make future pro-life legislation nearly impossible if passed.
issues.scripture: Lo, children are an heritage of the LORD: and the fruit of the womb is his reward.

issues.title: Virginia: Repeal Same-Sex Marriage Ban
issues.summary: Would remove the 2006 constitutional ban on same-sex marriage and replace it with language affirming marriage 'regardless of sex, gender, or race.' While the ban is currently unenforceable due to federal courts, this would permanently alter the state constitution.
issues.body:
This legislatively referred amendment would repeal the 2006 voter-approved ban on same-sex marriage and replace it with language requiring the state to recognize marriages 'regardless of sex, gender, or race.' The current ban was rendered unenforceable by the Fourth Circuit Court of Appeals in Bostic v. Schaefer (2014), which was affirmed by the Supreme Court's Obergefell decision.

Virginia would become the fifth state to vote on repealing a same-sex marriage ban from its constitution. The measure was approved twice by the General Assembly as required for constitutional amendments.
issues.analysis: While the existing ban is unenforceable, this amendment would permanently remove the state's constitutional affirmation of traditional marriage. Christians who hold to a biblical definition of marriage should be aware of this measure.
issues.scripture: Therefore shall a man leave his father and his mother, and shall cleave unto his wife: and they shall be one flesh.

issues.title: Virginia: Congressional Redistricting Amendment
issues.summary: Constitutional amendment allowing the legislature to conduct mid-decade redistricting of congressional districts in response to redistricting by other states. First ballot measure election of the 2026 cycle.
issues.body:
This amendment would allow the Virginia General Assembly to redraw congressional districts outside of the normal post-census redistricting cycle, specifically in response to mid-decade redistricting actions taken by other states.

The April 21, 2026 election is the first statewide ballot measure of the 2026 cycle. The amendment addresses growing concerns about partisan gerrymandering being conducted in other states that may disadvantage Virginia's representation.
issues.analysis: Redistricting is a matter of fair representation. Christians should evaluate whether this gives the legislature too much power to redraw maps for partisan advantage, or whether it is a reasonable response to other states' actions.
issues.scripture: The lot is cast into the lap; but the whole disposing thereof is of the LORD.

issues.title: Arkansas: Citizens Only Voting Amendment
issues.summary: Constitutional amendment specifying that only U.S. citizens may vote in any state or local election in Arkansas. Reinforces existing law with constitutional protection against future changes.
issues.body:
This legislatively referred amendment would add explicit language to the Arkansas Constitution stating that 'Only a citizen of the United States meeting the qualifications of an elector' may vote in state or local elections. While noncitizen voting is already prohibited under existing law, this amendment would elevate that prohibition to constitutional status.

The measure is part of a broader national trend of states constitutionally codifying citizenship requirements for voting. Requires a simple majority to pass, effective January 1, 2027.
issues.analysis: Ensuring that only lawful citizens participate in elections upholds the integrity of self-governance. This is a straightforward measure to constitutionally protect an existing standard.
issues.scripture: Righteousness exalteth a nation: but sin is a reproach to any people.

issues.title: Arkansas: Expanded Right to Keep and Bear Arms
issues.summary: Constitutional amendment expanding Second Amendment protections to explicitly include ammunition, firearm accessories, and firearm components. Strengthens existing gun rights beyond current constitutional language.
issues.body:
This amendment would alter the portion of the Arkansas Constitution that echoes the federal Second Amendment. The expanded language would clarify that the right to keep and bear arms extends to 'the possession and use of' ammunition, firearm accessories, and firearm components.

This measure is designed to preemptively protect against future attempts to restrict access to ammunition or accessories as an indirect means of limiting firearm ownership. Requires a simple majority to pass, effective January 1, 2027.
issues.analysis: The right of self-defense is grounded in natural law. This amendment strengthens protections for lawful firearm ownership by closing potential regulatory loopholes around ammunition and accessories.
issues.scripture: He that hath no sword, let him sell his garment, and buy one.

issues.title: North Carolina: Expanded Voter ID Requirement
issues.summary: Constitutional amendment extending photo ID requirements to all voters, including mail-in voters. Currently, ID is only required for in-person voting. A federal court recently upheld the state's existing voter ID law.
issues.body:
The North Carolina Require Voter Identification Amendment (SB 921) would expand the existing constitutional photo ID requirement to cover all forms of voting, not just in-person. As of 2024, photo voter ID was only required for in-person voting after years of legal challenges.

In 2018, voters approved a constitutional amendment creating a photo ID requirement (55.49% to 44.51%), but implementation was blocked by litigation until the NC Supreme Court reversed course in 2023. On March 26, 2026, a federal court upheld the state's voter ID law, providing legal momentum for this expansion.
issues.analysis: Extending voter ID to mail-in ballots closes a significant gap in election security. This measure builds on a voter-approved mandate from 2018 and strengthens the integrity of all forms of voting.
issues.scripture: Thou shalt not bear false witness against thy neighbour.

issues.title: Colorado: Biological Sex Classification for School Sports
issues.summary: Initiative requiring school athletic teams to be classified by biological sex (male, female, or coeducational). Would prohibit biologically male students from competing on female teams. One of several conservative measures certified for the Colorado ballot.
issues.body:
Colorado Initiative 109 would require school- and association-sponsored athletic teams to be classified in one of three categories based on biological sex: males/men/boys, females/women/girls, or coeducational/mixed. Teams designated for females would not be open to male students. Teams designated for males would not be open to female students unless no corresponding female team is offered.

This is part of a slate of conservative ballot measures in Colorado -- a traditionally blue state -- alongside measures on fentanyl penalties, human trafficking, immigration enforcement, and a ban on gender-affirming surgery for minors.
issues.analysis: Protecting fair competition for female athletes is a matter of justice and truth. God created male and female with distinct biological differences that are relevant to athletic competition.
issues.scripture: So God created man in his own image, in the image of God created he him; male and female created he them.

issues.title: Colorado: Ban Gender-Affirming Surgery for Minors
issues.summary: Initiative prohibiting healthcare professionals from performing surgeries on minors that alter biological sex characteristics. Would also ban use of state or federal funds for such procedures.
issues.body:
This citizen-initiated statute would prohibit healthcare professionals from performing any surgeries on minors that would alter biological sex characteristics. The measure also prohibits the use of state or federal funds for such surgeries.

Colorado would become one of the first blue states where voters directly decide this issue via ballot measure rather than legislation. The measure was certified alongside related initiatives on school sports and immigration enforcement.
issues.analysis: Children cannot consent to irreversible surgical alterations of their God-given bodies. Protecting minors from permanent, life-altering medical decisions is a fundamental duty of a just society.
issues.scripture: Train up a child in the way he should go: and when he is old, he will not depart from it.

issues.title: Colorado: Immigration Status Investigation & DHS Notification
issues.summary: Constitutional amendment requiring law enforcement to investigate immigration status of charged individuals and notify DHS if they are unlawfully present and charged with a violent crime or have a prior felony.
issues.body:
Colorado Initiative 95 would amend the state constitution to require state and local law enforcement to investigate the immigration status of individuals they charge with a crime. If the individual is not lawfully present or their status cannot be determined, and they are charged with a violent crime or have a prior felony conviction, law enforcement must notify the U.S. Department of Homeland Security.

This measure directly challenges Colorado's sanctuary-leaning policies and is part of a broader conservative ballot slate that includes measures on sports, gender surgery, fentanyl, and human trafficking.
issues.analysis: Nations have a legitimate right and duty to enforce their borders and laws. Requiring cooperation with federal immigration enforcement on violent criminals upholds the rule of law.
issues.scripture: Let every soul be subject unto the higher powers. For there is no power but of God: the powers that be are ordained of God.

issues.title: Colorado: Increased Fentanyl Penalties
issues.summary: Initiative establishing higher felony classifications and mandatory penalties for manufacturing, dispensing, selling, and possessing fentanyl. Responds to the ongoing fentanyl crisis.
issues.body:
Colorado Initiative 85 would establish higher felony classifications and mandatory penalties for the manufacturing, dispensing, sale, and possession of fentanyl. The measure responds to the devastating fentanyl crisis that has killed tens of thousands of Americans.

Colorado has been criticized by law enforcement for its relatively lenient drug penalties, and this measure would significantly strengthen consequences for fentanyl-related offenses.
issues.analysis: Protecting communities from the scourge of fentanyl is a matter of loving one's neighbor. Stronger penalties for those who profit from deadly substances serve justice and protect the vulnerable.
issues.scripture: Woe unto him that giveth his neighbour drink, that puttest thy bottle to him, and makest him drunken also.

issues.title: Colorado: Life Without Parole for Child Sex Trafficking
issues.summary: Initiative mandating life in prison without possibility of parole for persons convicted of human trafficking a child for sexual servitude. Elevates the offense from Class 2 to Class 1 felony.
issues.body:
Colorado Initiative 108 would increase the penalty for sexual trafficking of a minor from a Class 2 felony to a Class 1 felony, requiring a mandatory sentence of life in prison without the possibility of parole or release.

This measure reflects growing national concern over child exploitation and sex trafficking, and would make Colorado's penalties among the most severe in the nation for this crime.
issues.analysis: The protection of children from exploitation and abuse is among the highest moral obligations of any society. This measure ensures that those who traffic children for sexual servitude face the most severe possible consequences.
issues.scripture: But whoso shall offend one of these little ones which believe in me, it were better for him that a millstone were hanged about his neck, and that he were drowned in the depth of the sea.

issues.title: Washington: Biological Sex Verification for Female Sports
issues.summary: First statewide measure in U.S. history requiring biological sex verification for school sports eligibility. Would require healthcare provider verification based on reproductive anatomy, genetics, or testosterone levels.
issues.body:
The Washington Sex Verification Requirements for Female School Sports Initiative is the first statewide measure in American history related to biological sex requirements for school sports eligibility. The initiative would require schools and voluntary nonprofit athletics organizations to prohibit biologically male students from competing against biologically female students.

Biological sex must be verified by a healthcare provider based on reproductive anatomy, genetic makeup, or normal endogenously produced testosterone levels. The measure was backed by Let's Go Washington, a PAC founded by Brian Heywood. The Washington Legislature did not act on the initiative before the session adjourned on March 12, 2026, placing it automatically on the November ballot.
issues.analysis: This historic measure in a deep-blue state reflects the common-sense understanding that biological sex is a meaningful and immutable distinction. Protecting fair competition for girls and women upholds truth and justice.
issues.scripture: So God created man in his own image, in the image of God created he him; male and female created he them.

issues.title: Massachusetts: Firearm Regulations Veto Referendum
issues.summary: Veto referendum to repeal the 2024 'An Act Modernizing Firearm Laws' (HB 4885). A 'No' vote would repeal the restrictive gun law. Led by the Civil Rights Coalition.
issues.body:
The Massachusetts Firearm Regulations Referendum is a veto referendum targeting House Bill 4885 (Acts of 2024 Chapter 135), also known as 'An Act Modernizing Firearm Laws,' which was passed during the 2024 legislative session and took effect October 2, 2024.

In Massachusetts veto referendums, the campaign sponsors advocate for a 'No' vote, as a 'No' vote repeals the targeted legislation. The Civil Rights Coalition is leading the campaign to repeal the law, while Governor Maura Healey and the Massachusetts Coalition to Prevent Gun Violence support upholding it.

This is a critical gun rights battle in one of the nation's most restrictive states for firearms.
issues.analysis: The right to bear arms is a fundamental natural right. Massachusetts' restrictive gun laws burden law-abiding citizens while doing little to prevent criminal violence. Voting 'No' repeals the overreaching law.
issues.scripture: If a thief be found breaking up, and be smitten that he die, there shall no blood be shed for him.

issues.title: Massachusetts: Repeal Recreational Marijuana Sales
issues.summary: Initiative to repeal laws permitting recreational marijuana sales and home cultivation. Would decriminalize possession of up to one ounce but eliminate the commercial cannabis industry.
issues.body:
The Massachusetts Eliminate Recreational Marijuana Sales and Allow Limited Possession Initiative would repeal state laws permitting adults to purchase recreational marijuana from retail stores and cultivate cannabis at home. The measure would still allow possession of up to one ounce.

Polling shows the measure faces an uphill battle, with 63% of voters opposing it, including strong majorities of Democrats (74%) and Independents (69%). Nevertheless, the measure reflects growing concerns about the social and health impacts of commercial marijuana.
issues.analysis: While polling suggests this measure is unlikely to pass, it reflects legitimate concerns about the moral and social costs of legalized drug commerce. Christians should consider the effects of marijuana on families and communities.
issues.scripture: Wine is a mocker, strong drink is raging: and whosoever is deceived thereby is not wise.

issues.title: Idaho HJR 4: Block Citizen Marijuana Initiatives
issues.summary: Legislatively referred amendment that would permanently prevent citizens from using the ballot initiative process to legalize marijuana or other narcotics. Only the legislature could change drug laws.
issues.body:
Idaho HJR 4 would amend the state constitution to grant the Idaho Legislature exclusive authority over marijuana, narcotics, and psychoactive substances legislation. Citizens would be permanently barred from using the initiative process to legalize, decriminalize, or regulate these substances.

The measure specifically targets growing efforts to legalize medical and recreational marijuana in Idaho via citizen initiative. Two marijuana-related initiatives (one medical, one recreational) are simultaneously collecting signatures for the 2026 ballot. HJR 4 would not affect initiatives on the 2026 ballot itself, but would block all future efforts.
issues.analysis: While protecting Idaho from drug legalization is a worthy goal, permanently stripping citizens of their initiative rights on this topic raises questions about the proper limits of legislative power versus the people's right to self-governance.
issues.scripture: Be sober, be vigilant; because your adversary the devil, as a roaring lion, walketh about, seeking whom he may devour.

issues.title: Texas SB 8: Bathroom Bill (Women's Privacy Act)
issues.summary: Now in effect. Restricts bathroom and locker room access in government facilities, K-12 schools, and universities to biological sex assigned at birth. Violations subject to $25,000-$125,000 fines.
issues.body:
Texas Senate Bill 8, the Women's Privacy Act, went into effect December 4, 2025 after being signed by Governor Abbott on September 22, 2025. The law restricts access to bathrooms and locker rooms in government facilities, K-12 schools, and universities based on biological sex assigned at birth.

Facilities that violate the law face fines of $25,000 for a first offense and $125,000 for subsequent violations, counted daily. Six anti-trans bills passed into law and went into effect September 1, 2025, making Texas one of the most active states on these issues.
issues.analysis: Privacy and safety in intimate facilities is a legitimate concern. This law upholds the biological reality of sex differences and protects women and girls from exposure to biological males in private spaces.
issues.scripture: So God created man in his own image, in the image of God created he him; male and female created he them.

issues.title: Tennessee: Education Freedom Act (Universal ESA)
issues.summary: Tennessee passed the Education Freedom Act creating a universal ESA program with $7,000/student. Launches with 20,000 scholarships, half reserved for lower-income families and students with disabilities.
issues.body:
Tennessee passed the Education Freedom Act of 2025, creating a universal Education Savings Account (ESA) program providing $7,000 per student annually. The funds must first be used for tuition but can also cover other educational expenses.

The program starts with 20,000 scholarships, with half reserved for students from families earning up to 300% of the free and reduced-price lunch threshold and students with disabilities. This makes Tennessee one of the growing number of states offering universal school choice.
issues.analysis: Empowering parents to choose the best education for their children -- including faith-based schools -- is a victory for religious liberty and parental authority.
issues.scripture: And thou shalt teach them diligently unto thy children, and shalt talk of them when thou sittest in thine house.

issues.title: South Dakota: 60% Supermajority for Constitutional Amendments
issues.summary: Legislatively referred amendment requiring 60% voter approval to pass future constitutional amendments. Passed with near-unanimous Republican support (89 of 91). Aims to make progressive ballot measures harder to pass.
issues.body:
The South Dakota State Legislature referred a constitutional amendment that would raise the threshold for passing constitutional amendments from a simple majority (50%+1) to a 60% supermajority. The measure passed the legislature with near-unanimous Republican support (89 of 91 Republicans voted for it) while most Democrats opposed it.

This measure is part of a national trend of Republican-controlled legislatures seeking to make it harder to pass citizen-initiated constitutional amendments, often in response to progressive ballot measures on abortion, marijuana, and minimum wage.
issues.analysis: A higher threshold for constitutional changes provides stability and prevents slim majorities from making permanent constitutional alterations. However, it also makes it harder for citizens to amend their own constitution on any issue.
issues.scripture: Remove not the ancient landmark, which thy fathers have set.

issues.title: South Dakota: Proof of Citizenship for Voter Registration
issues.summary: South Dakota is one of three states (with Florida and Utah) to recently enact proof of citizenship laws for voter registration, joining the growing national movement for election integrity.
issues.body:
South Dakota has enacted legislation requiring proof of citizenship for voter registration, joining Florida and Utah as the latest states to pass such requirements. These states join Mississippi, which signed its own version of the SAVE America Act in 2025.

The federal SAVE Act, pushed by Congressional Republicans and supported by the Trump administration, would require proof of citizenship and photo ID to vote at the federal level. A March 2026 White House executive order on 'Ensuring Citizenship Verification and Integrity in Federal Elections' further advanced this agenda.
issues.analysis: Requiring proof of citizenship to register to vote is a basic safeguard for election integrity. Only lawful citizens should participate in choosing their representatives.
issues.scripture: Divers weights, and divers measures, both of them are alike abomination to the LORD.

issues.title: Utah: 60% Supermajority for Tax-Related Citizen Initiatives
issues.summary: Legislatively referred amendment requiring citizen-initiated measures to receive at least 60% voter approval for tax-related changes. Passed the legislature mostly along party lines (Republican-sponsored).
issues.body:
The Utah State Legislature referred a constitutional amendment requiring citizen-initiated measures that involve tax-related changes to receive at least 60% voter approval to pass. The Republican-sponsored resolution passed largely along party lines.

This targeted approach focuses specifically on preventing citizen initiatives from raising taxes with a bare majority, while leaving other types of initiatives at the standard threshold. Utah also enacted proof of citizenship requirements for voter registration.
issues.analysis: Requiring a higher bar for tax increases through citizen initiatives protects taxpayers from being burdened by slim-majority decisions. Fiscal restraint is consistent with principles of stewardship.
issues.scripture: For which of you, intending to build a tower, sitteth not down first, and counteth the cost, whether he have sufficient to finish it?

issues.title: Kansas: Elect Supreme Court Justices
issues.summary: Constitutional amendment to change Kansas Supreme Court justices from appointed to elected. Major judicial reform effort following the Kansas Supreme Court's 2019 ruling establishing a right to abortion in the state constitution.
issues.body:
The Kansas Legislature voted to send a constitutional amendment to the August 2026 ballot that would change how Kansas Supreme Court justices are selected -- from the current appointment system to direct elections by voters.

This measure is widely seen as a response to the Kansas Supreme Court's 2019 ruling in Hodes & Nauser v. Schmidt, which found a right to abortion in the Kansas Constitution. Conservative lawmakers argue that judicial elections would make justices more accountable to the people.

A separate amendment on the November ballot would also require that only U.S. citizens can vote in Kansas elections.
issues.analysis: An accountable judiciary is essential to representative government. When unelected judges create new constitutional rights from whole cloth, the people deserve the ability to choose their judges directly.
issues.scripture: Moreover thou shalt provide out of all the people able men, such as fear God, men of truth, hating covetousness.

issues.title: Kansas: Citizens-Only Voting Amendment
issues.summary: Constitutional amendment providing that only citizens of the United States can vote in Kansas state and local elections. Part of the national movement to constitutionally enshrine citizenship voting requirements.
issues.body:
Kansas voters will decide on a constitutional amendment providing that only U.S. citizens can vote in state and local elections. While noncitizen voting is already illegal under Kansas law, this amendment would elevate the prohibition to constitutional status, preventing future legislatures from changing it.

Kansas joins Arkansas and other states in pursuing constitutional citizenship requirements for voting in 2026.
issues.analysis: Constitutionally enshrining the requirement that only citizens may vote protects the fundamental principle that elections belong to the people of the nation.
issues.scripture: When the righteous are in authority, the people rejoice: but when the wicked beareth rule, the people mourn.

issues.title: Georgia: Religious Freedom Restoration Act Enacted
issues.summary: Georgia became the 30th state to pass its own Religious Freedom Restoration Act in 2025. The law matches federal RFRA language and allows individuals to recover legal costs when suing government over religious liberty infringements.
issues.body:
Georgia became the 30th state to enact its own Religious Freedom Restoration Act (RFRA) in 2025. The law matches federal RFRA language and includes additional provisions allowing plaintiffs in lawsuits against the government to recover their legal costs, and permitting judges to order governments to change laws or practices that infringe on religious beliefs.

This is a significant victory for religious liberty advocates who have sought state-level RFRA protections for years. The law provides important legal protections for people of faith against government overreach.
issues.analysis: Religious liberty is the first freedom. A state RFRA ensures that government cannot burden the free exercise of religion without demonstrating a compelling interest -- a vital protection for Christians and all people of faith.
issues.scripture: We ought to obey God rather than men.

issues.title: Oregon Referendum 120: Repeal Gas Tax / Fee Increases
issues.summary: Veto referendum to repeal portions of HB 3991 that would increase gas taxes, payroll taxes, and vehicle registration fees. Vote 'No' to repeal the tax increases. Filed by Republican legislators and taxpayer advocates.
issues.body:
Oregon Referendum 120 challenges provisions of House Bill 3991 that would increase the state's gas tax from $0.40 to $0.46, the transit payroll tax from 0.1% to 0.2%, and vehicle registration fees. HB 3991 was passed during a special session in September 2025 along party lines, with Democrats supporting and Republicans opposing.

The referendum was filed by State Sen. Bruce Starr (R), State Rep. Ed Diehl (R), and the Taxpayer Association of Oregon. In Oregon veto referendums, a 'No' vote overturns the targeted legislation. The vote is scheduled for May 19, 2026 -- earlier than most other ballot measures.
issues.analysis: Tax increases burden working families and should be subject to the people's consent. This referendum gives citizens the opportunity to reject tax hikes passed on a party-line vote.
issues.scripture: For which of you, intending to build a tower, sitteth not down first, and counteth the cost, whether he have sufficient to finish it?

issues.title: Nebraska: Personhood at Fertilization Amendment (Proposed)
issues.summary: Pro-life initiative to establish constitutional personhood at the moment of fertilization. Choose Life Now is collecting signatures after Nebraska voters approved a 12-week abortion ban in 2024. Would go beyond current law to ban all abortion.
issues.body:
Choose Life Now, the campaign that initiated Nebraska's 2024 Prohibit Abortions After the First Trimester Amendment, is collecting signatures for a new ballot initiative that would establish personhood beginning at fertilization. This would effectively ban all abortion in the state.

In 2024, Nebraska voters approved a constitutional amendment banning abortion after the first trimester (12 weeks), with exceptions for medical emergencies, rape, and incest. The new initiative would go significantly further.

Organizers must gather signatures from at least 10% of Nebraska voters, including 5% of registered voters in at least 38 of the state's 93 counties, by July 2026.
issues.analysis: Establishing personhood at fertilization is the most principled pro-life position, recognizing that every human being is created in the image of God from the moment of conception.
issues.scripture: Before I formed thee in the belly I knew thee; and before thou camest forth out of the womb I sanctified thee.

issues.title: Alaska: Repeal Ranked-Choice Voting
issues.summary: Initiative to eliminate the top-four primary and ranked-choice voting system implemented in 2022. Supported by President Trump, who called the system 'disastrous' and 'very fraudulent.'
issues.body:
Alaskans will vote on an initiative to eliminate the top-four primary and ranked-choice voting (RCV) system that was adopted by voters in 2020 and first used in 2022. The system allows the top four candidates from an open primary to advance to the general election, where voters rank candidates in order of preference.

President Trump has called Alaska's ranked-choice voting 'disastrous' and 'very fraudulent,' adding momentum to the repeal effort. Critics argue RCV confused voters and produced outcomes that don't reflect the will of the majority, while supporters say it reduces partisan extremism.
issues.analysis: Election systems should be simple, transparent, and produce clear outcomes that reflect the will of the people. Ranked-choice voting introduces complexity that can obscure rather than clarify the people's choice.
issues.scripture: Let your yea be yea; and your nay, nay.

issues.title: Indiana: Universal School Choice (No Income Cap)
issues.summary: Indiana eliminated the income cap for its Choice Scholarship voucher program, making it available to all students starting in 2026. One of the most established school choice programs in the nation is now universal.
issues.body:
Indiana eliminated the income cap for its Choice Scholarship voucher program, making it universally available to all students beginning in 2026. Indiana's voucher program has been one of the most established in the nation, and removing the income cap represents a major expansion.

This joins a growing wave of states (Texas, Tennessee, Idaho, and others) that have adopted or expanded universal school choice programs in 2025-2026.
issues.analysis: Universal school choice empowers all families -- not just the wealthy -- to choose the best educational environment for their children, including faith-based schools that integrate biblical values.
issues.scripture: And thou shalt teach them diligently unto thy children, and shalt talk of them when thou sittest in thine house.

sources.label: Ballotpedia - School Choice in the US

issues.title: Idaho: First Private School Choice Program (Tax Credit)
issues.summary: Idaho launched its first private school choice program through a refundable tax credit. Families receive up to $5,000/child for private educational expenses, with $7,500 for students with disabilities.
issues.body:
Idaho launched its first-ever private school choice program in 2025 through a refundable tax credit. Families can receive up to $5,000 per child for private educational expenses, with an enhanced $7,500 available for students with disabilities.

This is a historic first for Idaho, which had been one of the few red states without any form of private school choice program. The tax credit structure was used rather than a voucher or ESA to reduce political opposition.
issues.analysis: Even a modest first step toward school choice is significant. Idaho families now have some ability to direct educational funds toward schools that align with their values.
issues.scripture: Train up a child in the way he should go: and when he is old, he will not depart from it.

issues.title: Mississippi: Education Freedom Program (HB 2)
issues.summary: HB 2 passed the House creating universal ESAs (Magnolia Student Accounts) but was killed in the Senate Education Committee. Fight continues for school choice in Mississippi as neighboring states expand programs.
issues.body:
Mississippi's HB 2, the Education Freedom Program Act of 2026, would create Magnolia Student Accounts -- universal education savings accounts with a phase-in of 2,500 new seats per year, launching in 2027-2028 with a cap of 12,500 participants in the first year. Half of awards would be reserved for former public school students, prioritized by income.

The bill passed the Mississippi House but was killed in the Senate Education Committee in February 2026. As neighboring states like Texas, Tennessee, and Alabama expand school choice, pressure is mounting on Mississippi to follow suit.
issues.analysis: Mississippi parents deserve the same educational freedom that families in neighboring states now enjoy. Continued advocacy is needed to push this through the Senate.
issues.scripture: And, ye fathers, provoke not your children to wrath: but bring them up in the nurture and admonition of the Lord.

issues.title: Florida: Proof of Citizenship for Voter Registration
issues.summary: Florida enacted proof of citizenship requirements for voter registration, joining South Dakota and Utah as one of the latest states to pass such laws. Part of the SAVE America Act movement.
issues.body:
Florida Governor Ron DeSantis signed legislation requiring proof of citizenship for voter registration, making Florida the 10th state to enact such requirements. The law aligns with the federal SAVE America Act supported by the Trump administration.

Florida joins South Dakota and Utah in the latest wave of states strengthening citizenship verification for voting. Mississippi and other states signed similar legislation in 2025.
issues.analysis: Florida continues to lead on election integrity measures. Requiring proof of citizenship to register to vote is a common-sense safeguard for the democratic process.
issues.scripture: A false balance is abomination to the LORD: but a just weight is his delight.

issues.title: Wisconsin: Limit Governor's Partial Veto Power on Taxes
issues.summary: Constitutional amendment prohibiting the governor from using partial veto power to create or increase any tax or fee. Targets Governor Evers' creative use of the veto pen to alter legislation.
issues.body:
The Wisconsin State Legislature referred a constitutional amendment that would prohibit the governor from using the partial (line-item) veto power to create or increase any tax or fee. Wisconsin's partial veto power is among the broadest in the nation, and governors have used it to significantly alter the intent of passed legislation.

This is one of three amendments placed on the November 2026 ballot by the Republican-controlled legislature, and is widely seen as a response to Governor Tony Evers' use of creative partial vetoes.
issues.analysis: Preventing the executive from unilaterally creating or increasing taxes through veto manipulation upholds the constitutional principle that taxing power belongs to the legislature as the people's representatives.
issues.scripture: The king by judgment establisheth the land: but he that receiveth gifts overthroweth it.

issues.title: California: Changes to Initiative Process
issues.summary: Initiated amendment to allow ballot initiatives on odd-year and primary ballots, decrease required signatures, and levy a 5% tax on income above $10 million to fund initiative elections. Would significantly alter California's direct democracy process.
issues.body:
This initiated constitutional amendment in California would make several changes to the state's initiative process: allowing ballot initiatives to appear on odd-year and primary election ballots (not just November general elections), decreasing the number of required signatures to qualify measures, and levying a 5% tax on income above $10 million to fund the state cost of ballot initiative elections.

Conservative voters should be aware that lowering the signature threshold and expanding when initiatives can appear could make it easier for well-funded progressive groups to place measures on low-turnout elections.
issues.analysis: While expanding direct democracy sounds appealing, lowering barriers for ballot measures in California -- where progressive groups have deep funding advantages -- could lead to more radical policy changes through low-turnout elections.
issues.scripture: Where no counsel is, the people fall: but in the multitude of counsellors there is safety.

issues.title: Louisiana: Property Tax Assessment Level Income Increase
issues.summary: Constitutional amendment raising the income limit to qualify for property tax special assessment from $100,000 to $150,000. One of six amendments referred by the Louisiana legislature.
issues.body:
The Louisiana State Legislature referred six constitutional amendments to the 2026 ballot during the final week of the 2025 legislative session. Among them is an amendment that would raise the income limit to qualify for the property tax special assessment level from $100,000 to $150,000, providing property tax relief to more middle-income homeowners.

Louisiana's slate of six amendments represents one of the larger packages of referred measures in the 2026 cycle.
issues.analysis: Expanding property tax relief to more homeowners supports families and homeownership. Tax policy should support those who are building stable communities.
issues.scripture: But if any provide not for his own, and specially for those of his own house, he hath denied the faith, and is worse than an infidel.

issues.title: Federal: SAVE America Act (Proof of Citizenship to Vote)
issues.summary: Federal legislation requiring proof of citizenship and photo ID to vote. Passed the Senate in 2025. The Trump administration issued an executive order in March 2026 on citizenship verification. Multiple states are enacting their own versions.
issues.body:
The SAVE America Act (Safeguard American Voter Eligibility Act) is federal legislation requiring voters to provide proof of citizenship and photo ID to vote. The Senate passed a version in 2025, and President Trump issued an executive order in March 2026 titled 'Ensuring Citizenship Verification and Integrity in Federal Elections.'

Republican governors in Florida, Mississippi, South Dakota, and Utah have signed their own state versions. Multiple states have ballot measures in 2026 to constitutionally enshrine citizenship voting requirements (Arkansas, Kansas, Nevada, North Carolina).

This represents the most significant federal election integrity reform in decades.
issues.analysis: Ensuring that only citizens vote in federal elections is foundational to the legitimacy of representative government. Christians should support measures that uphold honest and fair elections.
issues.scripture: Ye shall do no unrighteousness in judgment, in meteyard, in weight, or in measure.

issues.title: Federal: School Choice Voucher Program (4+ New States)
issues.summary: Federal legislation creating a school choice voucher program that will bring private school choice to at least 4 new states that did not previously have programs. Passed the Senate in 2025.
issues.body:
The U.S. Senate passed a federal voucher program in 2025 that will bring private school choice to at least four new states. This federal program supplements the explosive growth of state-level school choice programs in Texas, Tennessee, Indiana, Idaho, and other states.

The federal program represents a historic expansion of educational freedom at the national level, complementing the state-level movement that has seen universal school choice programs adopted across the country.
issues.analysis: Federal support for school choice amplifies the state-level movement and ensures that parents in every state have some pathway to direct their children's education according to their values.
issues.scripture: Train up a child in the way he should go: and when he is old, he will not depart from it.

issues.title: Washington: Parental Rights in Public Schools Initiative
issues.summary: Companion initiative to the sports measure, establishing certain parental rights in public schools. Backed by Let's Go Washington PAC. Legislature did not act, placing it automatically on the November ballot.
issues.body:
Washington voters will decide on a parental rights initiative alongside the biological sex sports measure. Both initiatives were filed by Let's Go Washington, the PAC founded by Brian Heywood. The Washington State Legislature did not act on either initiative before the session adjourned on March 12, 2026, automatically placing them on the November 3 ballot.

The parental rights measure establishes certain parental rights within the public school system, part of a broader national movement asserting parents' role as the primary decision-makers in their children's education.
issues.analysis: Parental authority over children's education is a fundamental right rooted in Scripture. Schools serve families, not the other way around.
issues.scripture: And, ye fathers, provoke not your children to wrath: but bring them up in the nurture and admonition of the Lord.

issues.title: Alaska: New Campaign Contribution Limits
issues.summary: Initiative to establish new campaign contribution limits in Alaska. Certified for the November 2026 ballot alongside the ranked-choice voting repeal measure.
issues.body:
Alaska voters will decide on an initiative establishing new campaign contribution limits for state elections. This measure is on the ballot alongside the initiative to repeal ranked-choice voting.

Campaign finance reform is often framed as a good-government issue, but specific limits and regulations can have significant impacts on which candidates and causes can effectively compete.
issues.analysis: Campaign finance rules shape who can effectively participate in the political process. Christians should evaluate whether proposed limits would help or hinder candidates of faith and principle.
issues.scripture: The rich man's wealth is his strong city: the destruction of the poor is their poverty.

issues.title: Arkansas: Religious Exemptions for Nondiscrimination
issues.summary: Arkansas passed HB 1616 creating religious exemptions for nondiscrimination protections and HB 0207 allowing religious exemptions that could affect housing, healthcare, and employment for LGBTQ individuals.
issues.body:
The Arkansas legislature passed two significant religious exemption bills in 2025. HB 1616 creates religious exemptions for nondiscrimination protections, and HB 0207 allows religious exemptions that affect housing, healthcare, and employment decisions.

These laws provide important protections for people of faith who object to participating in activities that violate their sincerely held religious beliefs, particularly regarding sexual orientation and gender identity issues.
issues.analysis: Religious liberty protections are essential in a time when people of faith face increasing pressure to compromise their beliefs. These exemptions protect the right of Christians to live according to their convictions.
issues.scripture: We ought to obey God rather than men.

issues.title: Multiple States: School Chaplain Legislation
issues.summary: Lawmakers in Alabama, Florida, Georgia, Indiana, Iowa, Kansas, Maryland, Mississippi, Missouri, Nebraska, Ohio, Oklahoma, and Utah have introduced legislation allowing chaplains to serve in public schools.
issues.body:
A growing movement across state legislatures seeks to allow chaplains to serve in public schools. Bills have been introduced in Alabama, Florida, Georgia, Indiana, Iowa, Kansas, Maryland, Mississippi, Missouri, Nebraska, Ohio, Oklahoma, and Utah during the 2025-2026 legislative sessions.

These bills would allow trained chaplains to provide counseling and support services to students, often as a voluntary alternative to school counselors. Supporters argue that many students need spiritual support, while opponents raise concerns about separation of church and state.
issues.analysis: Allowing chaplains in schools provides students access to spiritual counsel and support. This represents an important expansion of religious presence in public education.
issues.scripture: The Lord is nigh unto them that are of a broken heart; and saveth such as be of a contrite spirit.

issues.title: Alabama Pledge of Allegiance and Student-Led Prayer Amendment (Nov 2026)
issues.summary: Legislatively referred constitutional amendment (HB 511) that would write the existing Pledge of Allegiance requirement and the existing right to student-initiated, student-led prayer into the Alabama Constitution. Both are already in state statute; the amendment elevates them to constitutional status.
issues.body:
What The Amendment Does

  • Requires public K-12 schools to conduct the Pledge of Allegiance at the start of each school day (already required by state statute).
  • Allows student-initiated, student-led prayer at the start of the school day (already permitted by state statute).
  • Moves both provisions from statute into the state constitution, making them harder for a future legislature to repeal.

How It Got On The Ballot

  • HB 511 introduced: Feb. 25, 2026 by State Rep. Reed Ingram (R-75).
  • House vote: 94-3 in favor (March 10, 2026). Republicans voted unanimously; Democrats split 19-3.
  • Senate vote: 30-0 in favor (April 8, 2026). Unanimous bipartisan passage.
  • Alabama requires a 60% supermajority in both chambers to refer a constitutional amendment; both votes cleared that bar comfortably.

Existing Federal Law Context

Student-led, voluntary prayer is already protected under U.S. Supreme Court precedent (Engel v. Vitale, Lee v. Weisman, Kennedy v. Bremerton). The amendment does not change federal constitutional limits on school-led or coerced prayer; it constitutionalizes the rights students already hold.
issues.analysis: This is a defensive amendment, not an expansion. The Pledge requirement and the protection for student-led prayer already exist in Alabama statute. Moving them into the state constitution makes them harder to undo in a future legislative session. Christian voters who value the freedom of their children to pray voluntarily at school should understand that the practical effect is preservation, not new rights. The near-unanimous bipartisan vote (94-3 House, 30-0 Senate) is the relevant tell: this is uncontroversial within Alabama's elected officials. Watch for the campaign-finance reports closer to the election to see whether any organized opposition materializes.
issues.scripture: Suffer the little children to come unto me, and forbid them not: for of such is the kingdom of God.
opponents: Three Alabama House Democrats voted no (94-3 House vote)
Rev. Julie Conrady, Unitarian Universalist Church of Birmingham — public testimony in opposition

issues.title: Iowa Two-Thirds Vote Requirement for Income Tax Increases Amendment (Nov 2026)
issues.summary: Legislatively referred constitutional amendment that would require a two-thirds supermajority vote of both chambers of the Iowa Legislature to pass any bill that raises an individual or corporate income tax rate or creates a new income tax. Cleared the two-session passage required for Iowa constitutional amendments in March 2024 and May 2026.
issues.body:
What The Amendment Does

  • Locks in a two-thirds (supermajority) legislative vote requirement to raise individual or corporate income tax rates.
  • Applies the same two-thirds threshold to any bill that creates a new income tax.
  • Current rule: a simple majority of each chamber can raise the rate. The amendment makes future increases substantially harder.

How It Got On The Ballot

Iowa requires the legislature to pass the same constitutional amendment in two consecutive sessions with an intervening election before it goes to voters.

  • 2023-2024 session: House 61-35 (March 26, 2024); Senate 31-13 (April 10, 2024). Party-line: every Yes was Republican; every No was Democrat.
  • 2025-2026 session: Senate 32-15 (April 15, 2025); House approved May 2, 2026.
  • Same party-line pattern in the second session. Now goes to voters Nov. 3, 2026 for ratification.

States With Similar Rules

Ballotpedia tracks 15 states that already have some form of legislative supermajority requirement to raise taxes. The most common threshold is two-thirds. Colorado (TABOR) goes further, requiring voter approval for any tax increase.
issues.analysis: This is a fiscal-restraint amendment. The practical effect is that future income tax increases will need broad legislative consensus rather than a single-vote majority. Christians who hold the biblical principle that the borrower is servant to the lender, and who view low and stable taxation as a check on overreach by civil magistrates, will read this favorably. The party-line votes in both sessions are the relevant signal: this is a contested change, not a consensus one — Democrats fear it locks in regressive limits on social spending, Republicans frame it as protecting working families from creeping tax hikes. Note: the amendment does NOT cap sales tax, property tax, or fee increases — only income tax. A simple majority can still raise those.
issues.scripture: The rich ruleth over the poor, and the borrower is servant to the lender.
supporters: Americans for Prosperity, Americans for Tax Reform
Iowa Association of Business and Industry
Iowans for Tax Relief Foundation, NFIB
opponents: State Sen. Tony Bisignano (D)
State Rep. David Jacoby (D)
Iowa Federation of Labor, AFL-CIO
Iowa Catholic Conference
Iowa Conference of the United Methodist Church
League of Women Voters of Iowa
Common Good Iowa

sources.label: Ballotpedia — Iowa Two-Thirds Income Tax Amendment (2026)

issues.title: Kentucky Limit on Governor's Pardon Power Amendment (Nov 2026)
issues.summary: Legislatively referred constitutional amendment (Senate Bill 10) that would prohibit the governor from issuing a pardon or commuting a sentence during a defined window around the gubernatorial election: 60 days before the election through the fifth Tuesday after it. Aimed at curbing the practice of last-minute lame-duck clemency.
issues.body:
What The Amendment Does

  • Amends Section 77 of the Kentucky Constitution to suspend the governor's pardon and commutation power during a window around the gubernatorial election.
  • The window: 60 days before the gubernatorial election through the fifth Tuesday after it.
  • Treason clemency and reprieves are unaffected; the limit applies only to pardons and sentence commutations.

How It Got On The Ballot

  • SB 10 introduced: Jan. 8, 2026 by State Sen. Chris McDaniel (R-23).
  • Senate vote: 36-0 (Jan. 20, 2026). Unanimous.
  • House vote: 82-12 with amendments (April 1, 2026). 98.1% of voting Republicans yes, 54.2% of voting Democrats yes.
  • Senate concurrence on amendments: 36-1.
  • Kentucky requires a 60% supermajority of each chamber to refer a constitutional amendment; both chambers cleared that bar.

Background

The amendment follows national attention to end-of-term mass clemency actions in Kentucky, most prominently the 2019 last-day pardons issued by then-Gov. Matt Bevin, which drew bipartisan criticism. The bill sponsor explicitly framed the measure as a response to that pattern.
issues.analysis: This is a check-on-executive-power amendment, and an unusually bipartisan one. Christians who believe civil magistrates exist to administer justice rather than to wield clemency as a parting political favor will read this favorably. The 36-0 and 36-1 Senate votes signal cross-party agreement that the governor's pardon power needs a guardrail during the politically charged period around a gubernatorial election. The narrower House vote (82-12) reflects some Democratic resistance, likely on separation-of-powers grounds. The amendment does NOT eliminate the pardon power; it imposes a roughly three-month window where it cannot be exercised. Treason cases and reprieves remain unaffected.
issues.scripture: When the righteous are in authority, the people rejoice: but when the wicked beareth rule, the people mourn.
opponents: 12 Kentucky House Democrats voted no
Ballotpedia had not located an organized opposition campaign as of April 2026

issues.title: Oklahoma State Question 846 — Voter ID Constitutional Amendment (Aug 2026)
issues.summary: Legislatively referred constitutional amendment (SJR 47) that would write a voter identification requirement directly into the Oklahoma Constitution, where it currently lives only in state statute. The form of ID would be defined by the state legislature. Going to voters on a special August 25, 2026 election ballot.
issues.body:
What The Amendment Does

  • Adds a constitutional requirement that voters provide proof of identity, as defined by the state legislature, when voting in any Oklahoma election.
  • Oklahoma already has a statutory voter ID requirement; this amendment moves that requirement into the constitution, where a future legislature cannot repeal it by simple majority.
  • The specific forms of acceptable ID continue to be set by the legislature in statute.

How It Got On The Ballot

  • SJR 47 introduced: March 3, 2026 by State Sen. Lonnie Paxton (R-23).
  • Senate vote: 39-8 (March 26, 2026). All 39 Republicans yes, all 8 Democrats no.
  • House vote: 80-13 (April 15, 2026). 76 of 80 Republicans yes, 4 Democrats yes, 13 Democrats no.
  • Both chambers also voted to place the measure on a special August 25, 2026 ballot rather than the November general election.
  • Attorney General review: the AG initially rejected the ballot title on May 7, 2026 as non-compliant; published a final rewritten title on May 29, 2026.

Background

Oklahoma is one of approximately 35 states with some form of voter ID requirement on the books. Most of those requirements exist only in statute. Moving the requirement into the state constitution sets a higher bar (a future constitutional amendment) for any reversal.
issues.analysis: This is an election-integrity amendment that hardens a status-quo statutory rule into the state constitution. Christians who believe godly civil order requires honest weights and measures — applied to elections as to commerce — will read this favorably. The party-line Senate vote (39-8 along R/D lines) is the relevant signal: this is contested. Republicans frame it as locking in a basic safeguard; Democrats frame it as constitutional clutter or as elevating a partisan policy choice. Note three caveats: (1) Oklahoma already has voter ID in statute, so the practical short-term change is small; (2) the legislature still defines which IDs count; (3) the August 25 special election typically has far lower turnout than November, which both sides understand and have priced into their strategies.
issues.scripture: A false balance is abomination to the LORD: but a just weight is his delight.
supporters: State Sen. Ty Burns (R), State Sen. Jonathan Wingard (R)
opponents: State Sen. Carri Hicks (D-40)
Oklahoma Senate Democrats (8-0 against)
13 Oklahoma House Democrats

sources.label: Ballotpedia — Oklahoma SQ 846 Voter ID Amendment (Aug 2026)

issues.title: Vermont Proposal 4 — Equal Protection of Law Amendment (Nov 2026)
issues.summary: Legislatively referred constitutional amendment that would add a new Article 23 to the Vermont Constitution guaranteeing equal protection under the law and prohibiting the denial of rights based on race, ethnicity, sex, religion, disability, sexual orientation, gender identity, gender expression, or national origin. Cleared the two-session passage required for Vermont constitutional amendments in 2024 and 2026.
issues.body:
What The Amendment Does

Adds the following new Article 23 to the Vermont Constitution:

That the people are guaranteed equal protection under the law. The State shall not deny equal treatment under the law on account of a person's race, ethnicity, sex, religion, disability, sexual orientation, gender identity, gender expression, or national origin. Nothing in this Article shall be interpreted or applied to prevent the adoption or implementation of measures intended to provide equality of treatment and opportunity for members of groups that have historically been subject to discrimination.

How It Got On The Ballot

Vermont requires constitutional amendments to pass two consecutive legislative sessions before going to voters.

  • 2023-2024 session: Senate 28-0 (April 23, 2024); House 141-4 (May 8, 2024).
  • 2025-2026 session: Senate 29-0 (March 11, 2026); House 128-14 (May 13, 2026).
  • Now goes to voters Nov. 3, 2026 for ratification.
issues.analysis: This amendment writes new categories — including gender identity and gender expression — into the Vermont Constitution as protected classes. Christians who hold the biblical anthropology that man is created male and female (Genesis 1:27) will read this with concern: a constitutional amendment is far harder to revise than a statute, and the inclusion of gender-identity language locks in a contested definition of personhood for the long term. The proposal's third sentence also constitutionally permits affirmative-action style measures for historically discriminated groups, which raises distinct questions about equal-protection coherence. Note the votes: passage was nearly unanimous in both Vermont sessions. Organized opposition has been minimal. Vermont voters who oppose elevating sexual-orientation and gender-identity language to constitutional status will need to organize, since the legislature is not the venue for reversal.
issues.scripture: And God created man to his own image: to the image of God he created him: male and female he created them.
opponents: State Rep. Michael Boutin (R Washington-3)
State Rep. Zachary Harvey (R Rutland-3)

issues.title: Arizona Designate Drug Cartels as Terrorist Organizations Measure (Nov 2026)
issues.summary: Legislatively referred state statute (HCR 2055) that would direct Arizona to recognize transnational drug cartels as terrorist organizations and authorize the state Department of Homeland Security to use available tools to confront them. The legislature sent it straight to voters after Gov. Katie Hobbs (D) vetoed a similar bill in 2023.
issues.body:
What The Measure Does

  • Declares that transnational drug cartels operating in Arizona are terrorist organizations.
  • Authorizes the Arizona Department of Homeland Security to use available state tools to address the threat the cartels pose — aimed at fentanyl trafficking, human smuggling, and cross-border violence.
  • If approved, Arizona would be the first state to make this designation by a vote of the people rather than by statute signed by a governor.

How It Got On The Ballot

  • House vote: 32-27 in favor (Feb. 26, 2025).
  • Senate vote: 16-13 in favor (June 27, 2025).
  • The votes split along party lines — Republicans in favor, Democrats opposed.
  • Because the measure was referred to the ballot by concurrent resolution, the governor cannot veto it; the decision rests with voters.

Background

A 2023 bill carrying a similar cartel-terrorism designation was vetoed by Gov. Hobbs. Referring the question directly to voters is how supporters routed around that veto. The designation is a state-law label and does not change the separate federal Foreign Terrorist Organization process run by the U.S. State Department.
issues.analysis: The drug cartels moving fentanyl and trafficking human beings across Arizona's southern border are, in plain terms, organized killers. Naming them what they are is reasonable, and the practical question for a RESOLUTE voter is narrower: does the state-law label actually give Arizona authorities new, usable tools, or is it mostly a statement? The text leans toward directing existing agencies rather than creating sweeping new powers, so weigh it as a real-but-modest step, not a border cure-all. The party-line votes tell you this is contested at the Capitol even though border security polls well across the aisle. A 'yes' aligns with protecting families and upholding the magistrate's God-given duty to restrain evildoers; just keep expectations honest about what a state designation can and cannot do on its own.
issues.scripture: For he is the minister of God to thee for good. But if thou do that which is evil, be afraid; for he beareth not the sword in vain.
opponents: Arizona legislative Democrats (party-line no)
