issues.title: Arizona Local Grocery Tax Cap and Voter-Approval Measure (Nov 2026)
issues.summary: Legislatively referred state statute (HCR 2021) that would cap any local sales tax on groceries at 2% of the base price and require voter approval before a city or local tax jurisdiction could raise its grocery tax. Sponsored by Rep. Leo Biasiucci (R-30).
issues.body:
What The Measure Does

  • Limits the rate a city or local tax jurisdiction may charge on the sale of food for home consumption to 2% of the base price.
  • Requires voter approval before any local grocery-tax increase takes effect.
  • Applies to municipal transaction privilege (sales) taxes on groceries — Arizona's state government already does not tax groceries.

How It Got On The Ballot

  • Sponsor: Rep. Leo Biasiucci (R-30), introduced Jan. 27, 2025.
  • House vote: 32-27 in favor (March 5, 2025).
  • Senate vote: 23-5 in favor (June 20, 2025).
  • Present Republicans voted for it; about 68% of Democrats voted against.

Context

Many Arizona cities levy their own sales tax on groceries even though the state does not. This measure does not abolish those local taxes; it caps them and puts future increases to a vote of the people in that jurisdiction.
issues.analysis: Taxing the family grocery bill falls hardest on households with the least margin, so a cap plus a requirement that local officials ask voters before raising it is a straightforward stewardship win — it keeps a recurring, regressive tax from creeping upward without consent. Be clear-eyed about scope: this caps and gates local grocery taxes rather than ending them, and cities will warn about lost revenue. That trade-off is the honest debate. For a RESOLUTE voter who believes government should be answerable to the governed and should not quietly squeeze the poor, requiring a vote before the tax on bread goes up is consistent with both just-weights economics and accountable government.
issues.scripture: A false balance is abomination to the LORD: but a just weight is his delight.
opponents: Most Arizona legislative Democrats (about 68% voted no)
Some city/local officials concerned about lost local revenue

issues.title: Arizona Prohibit Taxes or Fees on Vehicle Miles Traveled Amendment (Nov 2026)
issues.summary: Legislatively referred constitutional amendment (SCR 1004) that would prohibit the state and any local government from imposing a tax or fee based on the number of miles traveled in a motor vehicle, and from limiting how many miles a person may drive. Sponsored by Sen. Jake Hoffman (R-15); the first measure certified for the 2026 ballot.
issues.body:
What The Amendment Does

  • Prohibits Arizona and its political subdivisions from imposing any tax or fee calculated on vehicle miles traveled (a 'VMT' tax).
  • Prohibits state or local government from limiting the number of miles an individual may travel in a motor vehicle.
  • Writes both prohibitions into the Arizona Constitution, where a future legislature could not repeal them on its own.

How It Got On The Ballot

  • Sponsor: Sen. Jake Hoffman (R-15), introduced Dec. 16, 2024.
  • Senate vote: 17-12 (17 Republicans yes, 12 Democrats no).
  • House vote: 31-0 (31 Republicans yes; 27 Democrats and 2 Republicans not voting).
  • Supporters say no other state constitution currently bars VMT taxes or mileage limits, so this would be the first of its kind.

Context

VMT taxes are discussed nationally as a way to replace declining gas-tax revenue as more drivers switch to electric vehicles. No Arizona VMT tax or mileage limit is currently in force; this amendment is preemptive — it forecloses the option before any such proposal advances.
issues.analysis: This amendment is part tax policy, part liberty. The tax half is clean stewardship: a per-mile tax tracks how far you drive and tends to fall on rural families and working people who have no choice but to cover distance. Barring it constitutionally is a defensible guard against a future, hard-to-track tax. The 'no limiting miles traveled' half is preemptive — no Arizona government is proposing to ration your driving today, so weigh that clause as insurance against a hypothetical rather than a present fight, and decide whether you want freedom of movement locked into the constitution. The lopsided House vote (31-0, with most Democrats declining to vote rather than voting no) suggests opponents chose to sit it out rather than defend a VMT tax openly. For a RESOLUTE voter, a 'yes' protects family budgets and personal mobility; just go in knowing one half addresses a real proposal and the other guards against a future one.
issues.scripture: A prudent man foreseeth the evil, and hideth himself: but the simple pass on, and are punished.
opponents: Arizona legislative Democrats (12 Senate no votes; most House Democrats declined to vote)

issues.title: Florida Homestead Tax Exemption & Property-Tax Relief Amendment (Nov 2026)
issues.summary: Legislatively referred constitutional amendment (HJR 1F) that would raise the homestead exemption on non-school property taxes from $25,000 to $150,000 in 2027 and $250,000 in 2028, index it to inflation, cut the annual assessment cap on non-homestead property from 10% to 5%, and place new restrictions on local-government spending. Referred to voters in the June 2026 special session; needs 60% to pass.
issues.body:
What The Amendment Does

  • Raises the homestead exemption that applies to county, city, and special-district (non-school) property taxes from $25,000 to $150,000 in 2027, then to $250,000 in 2028, with inflation indexing beginning in 2029.
  • Leaves the existing $25,000 exemption for school-district taxes unchanged — so a homeowner would effectively carry two exemptions.
  • Reduces the annual assessment-increase cap on non-homestead property from 10% to 5%, starting January 1, 2027.
  • Adds restrictions on how much county and municipal governments can spend in specified categories, and includes a five-year phase-in for new Florida residents.

How It Got On The Ballot

  • The Legislature referred it in the June 1–3, 2026 special session.
  • House vote: 75-26 in favor.
  • Senate vote: 30-9 in favor.
  • Like all Florida constitutional amendments, it needs 60% voter approval to take effect.
issues.analysis: This is a real, substantial tax cut for Florida homeowners, and the structure is honest about what it touches: it lowers the bite on county and city taxes while deliberately leaving school funding alone. For a family trying to hold onto a home, raising the exemption to $150,000 and then $250,000 is meaningful relief, and dropping the non-homestead assessment cap from 10% to 5% slows how fast the tax bill climbs on everything else. The harder question for a RESOLUTE voter is downstream: property taxes fund local services, so weigh whether your county can absorb the cut without leaning on new fees or debt, and read the spending-restriction language as a feature, not a footnote. A 'yes' favors letting households keep more of what they earn and tightening the leash on local spending; just go in clear-eyed that lost revenue lands somewhere.
issues.scripture: A good man leaveth an inheritance to his children's children: and the wealth of the sinner is laid up for the just.
supporters: Florida legislative Republicans (referred it 75-26 House / 30-9 Senate)
Gov. Ron DeSantis (R) and property-tax-relief advocates
opponents: Some local-government officials and associations concerned about lost county/city revenue

issues.title: Florida Budget Stabilization Fund Amendment (Nov 2026)
issues.summary: Legislatively referred constitutional amendment that would raise the cap on Florida's budget stabilization (rainy-day) fund from 10% to 25% of net general revenue and require an annual transfer of the lesser of $750 million or 25% of collections, while letting the Legislature suspend transfers under certain conditions. Needs 60% to pass.
issues.body:
What The Amendment Does

  • Increases the maximum size of Florida's budget stabilization fund from 10% to 25% of net general revenue.
  • Requires an annual transfer into the fund of whichever is lesser: $750 million, or 25% of the prior year's collections.
  • Allows the Legislature to suspend the required transfer under specified conditions.

How It Got On The Ballot

  • The Legislature referred the amendment to voters for the November 3, 2026 ballot.
  • Like all Florida constitutional amendments, it needs 60% voter approval to take effect.
issues.analysis: This is the quiet companion to the property-tax cut on the same ballot: a larger rainy-day fund. A bigger reserve is plain prudence — it lets the state weather a downturn or a hurricane season without scrambling, and it is the public-finance version of a household keeping savings on hand. The fair counter-question is whether parking up to a quarter of general revenue in reserve ties money up that could go to taxpayers or current needs, and the escape hatch — letting the Legislature suspend transfers — means the discipline is real only as long as lawmakers honor it. For a RESOLUTE voter, this leans stewardship-positive and low-drama; the case for 'yes' is simply that a government, like a family, should not spend to the edge of its income.
issues.scripture: There is treasure to be desired and oil in the dwelling of the wise; but a foolish man spendeth it up.
supporters: Florida legislators backing a larger state reserve

issues.title: Florida Agriculture & Agritourism Property-Tax Exemption Amendment (Nov 2026)
issues.summary: Legislatively referred constitutional amendment that would exempt tangible personal property — farm equipment and tools typically found on agricultural land and used for farming or agritourism by the landowner or leaseholder — from property taxes. Needs 60% to pass.
issues.body:
What The Amendment Does

  • Exempts tangible personal property from property taxes when it is the kind of equipment typically present on agricultural land and is used for farming or agritourism.
  • Applies whether the property is used by the landowner or by a leaseholder working the land.

How It Got On The Ballot

  • The Legislature referred the amendment to voters for the November 3, 2026 ballot.
  • Like all Florida constitutional amendments, it needs 60% voter approval to take effect.
issues.analysis: Farm equipment is a working tool, not idle wealth, and taxing the tractor a family needs to bring in a harvest is the kind of friction that pushes small agriculture toward consolidation or closure. Exempting that equipment is a targeted relief that lines up with stewarding the land and keeping family farms viable. The narrow watch-item is the 'agritourism' clause: it is sensible for a real working farm that hosts visitors, but loosely drawn agritourism language can become a side door for non-farm property to claim a farm break — so the value of a 'yes' depends on how tightly the term is applied in practice. On balance this is a modest, pro-family, pro-stewardship measure rather than a sweeping one.
issues.scripture: Be thou diligent to know the state of thy flocks, and look well to thy herds.
supporters: Florida legislators backing agricultural tax relief

issues.title: Georgia Conservation-Use Acreage Limit Amendment (Nov 2026)
issues.summary: Legislatively referred constitutional amendment that would raise the maximum acreage one owner can enroll in Georgia's Conservation Use Valuation Assessment (CUVA) program — which taxes qualifying farm and timber land at its conservation-use value rather than fair-market value — from 2,000 to 4,000 acres. It would be the first acreage-limit increase since the program began.
issues.body:
What The Amendment Does

  • Increases the maximum acreage per owner that can qualify for Georgia's Conservation Use Valuation Assessment from 2,000 to 4,000 acres.
  • CUVA lets bona fide agricultural and timber land be taxed on its current-use value instead of its higher fair-market value, in exchange for a covenant to keep it in that use.
  • If approved, it would be the first increase to the acreage cap since the conservation-use program was established.

How It Got On The Ballot

  • The Georgia General Assembly referred the amendment to voters for the November 3, 2026 ballot.
  • A simple majority of votes statewide is required to ratify it.
issues.analysis: Conservation-use assessment is a sound idea: it keeps farm and timber land from being taxed out of existence by suburban land values, which protects both food production and open ground. Doubling the cap to 4,000 acres extends that protection to larger holdings. The honest tension is who benefits at the margin — raising the ceiling helps bigger landowners most, and a RESOLUTE voter who values the small family farm should ask whether this strengthens working agriculture broadly or mainly eases the tax bill on large estates. There is no obvious moral red flag here; it is a question of how far to extend a stewardship-minded break. If you trust that the land stays in genuine agricultural or timber use under the program's covenant, a 'yes' is defensible.
issues.scripture: The king himself is served by the field.
supporters: Georgia Farm Bureau and timber/agriculture landowners
opponents: Critics who argue the higher cap chiefly benefits the largest landowners

issues.title: Georgia Next Generation 9-1-1 Fund Amendment (Nov 2026)
issues.summary: Legislatively referred constitutional amendment that would authorize the General Assembly to create a dedicated Georgia Next Generation 9-1-1 Fund and direct revenue to upgrading the state's emergency-call (911) systems. It passed both the state Senate and House unanimously before going to voters.
issues.body:
What The Amendment Does

  • Authorizes the Georgia General Assembly to create a Next Generation 9-1-1 Fund and dedicate revenue to it.
  • The fund is aimed at modernizing the state's 911 emergency-call infrastructure (the shift to IP-based 'Next Generation 911' that handles texts, data, and more precise location).

How It Got On The Ballot

  • The state Senate approved it unanimously on March 31, and the House approved it unanimously as well.
  • The amendment was referred to voters for the November 3, 2026 ballot; a simple majority statewide is required to ratify it.
issues.analysis: Restraining evil and protecting life is a core, legitimate task of the civil magistrate, and a working 911 system is one of the plainest forms that takes — when a family dials for help, the call has to connect and find them. Dedicating revenue to modern emergency-call infrastructure is squarely within that duty, and the unanimous, bipartisan vote signals there is no real controversy about the need. The only stewardship question worth holding is the one that applies to any dedicated fund: a constitutional lockbox is harder to misuse but also harder to redirect if priorities change, so weigh whether you want this money walled off by the constitution rather than set yearly in the budget. For most RESOLUTE voters this is an easy, low-risk 'yes' on a genuine public-safety need.
issues.scripture: If thou forbear to deliver them that are drawn unto death, and those that are ready to be slain.
supporters: Georgia General Assembly (unanimous in both chambers)

issues.title: Georgia Nonpartisan Probate Judge Elections Amendment (Nov 2026)
issues.summary: Legislatively referred constitutional amendment (House Resolution 251) that would require all of Georgia's county probate judges to be elected on a nonpartisan basis rather than in partisan elections. It drew broad bipartisan support — about 94% of Republicans and 85% of Democrats in the General Assembly voted yes.
issues.body:
What The Amendment Does

  • Requires that elections for county probate judges in Georgia be conducted on a nonpartisan basis — candidates would appear without a party label.
  • Probate judges in Georgia handle wills and estates, guardianships, marriage and firearms licenses, and in many counties election administration and certain misdemeanor matters.

How It Got On The Ballot

  • Both chambers passed House Resolution 251; roughly 94.3% of Republicans and 85.4% of Democrats voted in favor.
  • The amendment was referred to voters for the November 3, 2026 ballot; a simple majority statewide is required to ratify it.
issues.analysis: A judge's job is to apply the law without fear or favor, and stripping the party label off probate-judge races fits the biblical standard that justice should not respect persons — the question on the bench is what the law and the facts require, not which team the judge plays for. Supporters across the aisle, including the state's chief justice and two former governors of both parties, make the same case. The honest counter-argument, which a careful voter should hold, is that party labels give voters a quick shorthand about a candidate's judicial philosophy, and removing them can leave low-information down-ballot races even harder to judge. On balance this leans toward 'yes' for anyone who wants courts seen as impartial; just know that nonpartisan does not mean apolitical, and you will have to do more homework on the candidates themselves.
issues.scripture: Ye shall do no unrighteousness in judgment: thou shalt not respect the person of the poor, nor honour the person of the mighty: but in righteousness shalt thou judge thy neighbour.
opponents: Some legislators who preferred keeping party labels on judicial races (minority no votes)

issues.title: Hawaii Senate Judicial-Appointment Review Window Amendment (Nov 2026)
issues.summary: Legislatively referred constitutional amendment (Senate Bill 121) extending the Hawaii Senate's window to review and act on a judicial appointment from 30 days to 60 days for appointments made between April 1 and December 31, when the Senate is often out of session. It cleared the Legislature with near-unanimous support and goes to voters as a yes/no question.
issues.body:
What The Amendment Does

  • Extends the time the Hawaii Senate has to confirm or reject a judicial appointment from 30 to 60 days.
  • The longer window applies only to appointments made April 1 through December 31, the stretch when the Senate is frequently not in session.
  • Sponsors say it lets the Senate vet nominees without convening a costly special session just to beat the 30-day clock.

How It Got On The Ballot

  • Senate Bill 121 (2025) was referred to voters by the Legislature.
  • Reported chamber votes: Senate 25-0 (March 4, 2025) and House 42-6 (April 4, 2025).
  • A simple statewide majority is required to ratify it on the November 3, 2026 ballot.
issues.analysis: A judge should be examined before he is confirmed, not waved through against a clock, and giving the Senate a real chance to search out a nominee's record is the cautious, defensible side of this. Folly is answering a matter before you have heard it. The honest counter a careful voter should hold is that a longer review window can also mean longer judicial vacancies and more leverage for the Senate to stall a nominee it simply dislikes. This is housekeeping, not a culture-war fight — it passed with almost no opposition — so weigh it on the merits: do you want appointments vetted more slowly and carefully, or filled faster? On balance it leans 'yes' for anyone who values deliberation over speed on the bench.
issues.scripture: He that answereth a matter before he heareth it, it is folly and shame unto him.
opponents: No organized opposition; the Hawaii Judiciary raised a neutral concern about prolonged vacancies

issues.title: Hawaii RISE Infrastructure Bonds Amendment (Nov 2026)
issues.summary: Legislatively referred constitutional amendment (Senate Bill 3219) that would let counties and other local subdivisions issue 'Resilient Infrastructure for Shelter and Equity' (RISE) bonds for public-works, housing, and community-development projects — payable from real-property taxes and exempt from the counties' debt-limit calculations.
issues.body:
What The Amendment Does

  • Amends Article VII (Sections 12 and 13) to authorize counties and political subdivisions to issue 'RISE' bonds.
  • The bonds would finance public-works, housing, and community-development projects.
  • They would be payable from real-property taxes and would NOT count against a county's constitutional debt limit.

How It Got On The Ballot

  • Senate Bill 3219 was referred to voters by the Legislature, approved May 6, 2026.
  • Reported chamber votes: Senate 25-0 and House 50-0 (one member not voting).
  • A simple statewide majority is required to ratify it on the November 3, 2026 ballot.
issues.analysis: There is a real case for fixing crumbling infrastructure and building more housing, and bonds are an ordinary tool for it. But the heart of this amendment is that it lets counties borrow OUTSIDE their constitutional debt limit — and that is exactly the part a steward should slow down on. The borrower is servant to the lender, and a debt ceiling exists to keep government from quietly mortgaging the next generation's property taxes. Backers point to genuine needs; the Tax Foundation of Hawaii warns that taxpayers stay on the hook if the financed projects don't pay off as promised. Lean cautious here: a 'yes' is a vote to loosen a guardrail on public debt, so be sure you trust your county to use that room wisely before you grant it.
issues.scripture: The rich ruleth over the poor, and the borrower is servant to the lender.
opponents: Tax Foundation of Hawaii (warns government stays liable if projects underperform)

issues.title: Idaho English as Official State Language Amendment (Nov 2026)
issues.summary: Legislatively referred constitutional amendment (House Joint Resolution 6) that would write English into the Idaho Constitution as the state's official language. English is already Idaho's official language by statute; this would elevate that designation to the constitution itself.
issues.body:
What The Amendment Does

  • Adds English as Idaho's official state language to the state constitution.
  • English is already designated the official language in Idaho statute; this moves the designation into the constitution, where a future legislature cannot repeal it by ordinary law.
  • The measure is symbolic and declaratory rather than a change to specific services.

How It Got On The Ballot

  • House Joint Resolution 6, introduced March 7, 2025 by Rep. Dale Hawkins (R-2B).
  • Reported chamber votes: House 59-8 (March 13, 2025) and Senate 30-5 (March 25, 2025).
  • The Legislative Council finalized the ballot language in June 2026; a simple statewide majority is required to ratify it on the November 3, 2026 ballot.
issues.analysis: A shared language is a real good — Paul's point that words must be 'easy to be understood' or no one knows what is spoken applies to a self-governing people too: you cannot deliberate together if you cannot understand one another. So the symbolism here is not nothing. The honest part of the ledger is that this changes little in practice — English is already official by statute, and the amendment names no service it would alter — so a voter should not expect it to 'do' much beyond making the statement constitutionally permanent. Critics call it unnecessary or unwelcoming; supporters call it a plain affirmation of a common civic tongue. This is a values vote with low practical stakes: decide whether you want that statement locked into the constitution, and don't oversell either its harm or its effect.
issues.scripture: Except ye utter by the tongue words easy to be understood, how shall it be known what is spoken? for ye shall speak into the air.
opponents: A small bloc of legislative Democrats (minority no votes)

issues.title: Indiana Bailable Offenses and Substantial Risk Amendment (Nov 2026)
issues.summary: Legislatively referred constitutional amendment (Senate Joint Resolution 1) that would add a 'substantial risk' standard to Indiana's bail clause — letting a court deny bail when the accused poses a substantial risk to another person or the community and no release condition would protect the public. Today only murder and treason allow denial of bail.
issues.body:
What The Amendment Does

  • Adds a 'substantial risk' standard to the Indiana Constitution's bail clause: offenses other than murder or treason remain bailable unless the accused poses a substantial risk to any other person or to the community.
  • Lets a court deny pretrial release when the presumption of guilt is strong and the state proves no condition of release would protect the public.
  • Expands judicial authority to detain a defendant before trial beyond the current murder-and-treason exceptions.

How It Got On The Ballot

  • Senate Joint Resolution 1. Indiana allows no citizen initiative; an amendment must pass two separately elected General Assemblies, then go to voters.
  • Second-passage votes (2026 session): Senate 43-2 and House 75-11; the resolution cleared the General Assembly February 17, 2026.
  • A simple statewide majority is required to ratify it on the November 3, 2026 ballot.
issues.analysis: Government bears the sword to be a terror to evil works and to protect the innocent, and there is a real, biblical case for keeping a genuinely dangerous defendant off the street while he awaits trial. That is the strength of this amendment. But the weight on the other side is just as serious: a man is presumed innocent until proven guilty, and 'substantial risk' is a phrase a judge must interpret — broaden it too far and you jail people who have been convicted of nothing. The safeguard written into the text is that the state must prove no release condition would work, which is meant to keep this narrow. Weigh it honestly: a 'yes' trades some pretrial liberty for community safety. Whether that trade is just depends on trusting Indiana's courts to apply 'substantial risk' soberly, not as a rubber stamp.
issues.scripture: For he is the minister of God to thee for good. But if thou do that which is evil, be afraid; for he beareth not the sword in vain.
opponents: Sen. Greg Taylor (D-33)
ACLU of Indiana
The Bail Project

issues.title: Indiana City and Town Court Judge Residency Amendment (Nov 2026)
issues.summary: Legislatively referred constitutional amendment (House Joint Resolution 6) that would let a city or town court judge live either in the county where the court sits or in the closest bordering county — loosening the current rule that the judge reside in the specific city or town the court serves.
issues.body:
What The Amendment Does

  • Permits a city or town court judge to reside in (1) the county where the court is located, or (2) the bordering county closest to that city or town.
  • Loosens the current constitutional requirement that the judge live in the specific city or town of the court.
  • Aims to widen the pool of eligible judges, especially for small jurisdictions near county lines.

How It Got On The Ballot

  • House Joint Resolution 6. Indiana amendments must pass two separately elected General Assemblies, then go to voters.
  • Reported votes: first passage (2023) House 90-0 and Senate 48-0; second passage (2025) House 81-7.
  • A simple statewide majority is required to ratify it on the November 3, 2026 ballot.
issues.analysis: This is plumbing, not politics — it passed all but unanimously — and the practical case is sound: a small town near a county line may simply not have a qualified, willing judge living inside its limits, and a slightly wider residency rule keeps those courts staffed. Scripture's pattern for judges is able, God-fearing men set over the people to judge at all seasons; getting a competent judge on the bench serves that end. The honest caution is small but real: the more distance you allow between a judge and the community he serves, the less he may know its character and circumstances firsthand. Here the loosening is modest — same county or the nearest bordering one — so the trade is minor. Decide it on practicality; there is no hidden agenda to hunt for in this one.
issues.scripture: Moreover thou shalt provide out of all the people able men, such as fear God, men of truth, hating covetousness; and place such over them, to be rulers... and let them judge the people at all seasons.
opponents: A small minority of no votes; no organized opposition identified

issues.title: Maryland Eminent Domain for Highway and Transit Purposes Amendment (Nov 2026)
issues.summary: Legislatively referred constitutional amendment (HB 1081 / SB 947) authorizing the General Assembly to enact laws letting the State Roads Commission or the Maryland Transit Administration (MTA) acquire private property by 'quick-take' condemnation for highway or transit purposes, with fair-market-value compensation to the owner.
issues.body:
What The Amendment Does

  • Authorizes the legislature to pass laws allowing the State Roads Commission or the Maryland Transit Administration to take private property for highway or transit purposes.
  • Extends 'quick-take' condemnation authority — the government can take possession of the property before the final compensation amount is settled in court.
  • Requires that the owner be paid fair market value.

How It Got On The Ballot

  • Legislatively referred constitutional amendment (Maryland has no citizen-initiative process for amendments; a 60% supermajority of each chamber is required — 85 of 141 in the House, 29 of 47 in the Senate).
  • Cross-filed as HB 1081 and SB 947. Reported chamber votes (Ballotpedia): HB 1081 — House 97-36, Senate 45-1; SB 947 — Senate 44-0, House 105-32. The supermajority threshold was met.
  • A simple statewide majority is required to ratify it on the November 3, 2026 ballot.
issues.analysis: Eminent domain is one of the oldest tests of whether a government honors the property of its people. Scripture takes private inheritance seriously — Naboth would not sell his fathers' vineyard, and it was wicked Ahab and Jezebel, not a righteous king, who seized it. This amendment widens the state's power to take land for roads and transit and to do so by 'quick-take,' meaning the bulldozers can arrive before the courthouse has fixed the price. The stated compensation is fair market value, which is the constitutional floor; the real questions a discerning citizen should ask are whether 'transit purposes' becomes a loophole for taking homes and small businesses for politically favored projects, and whether the owner's leverage collapses once the government already holds the deed. Support the rights of the landowner; demand that any taking be truly necessary, narrowly drawn, and fully and promptly paid.
issues.scripture: And Naboth said to Ahab, The LORD forbid it me, that I should give the inheritance of my fathers unto thee.
supporters: Maryland Department of Transportation / Maryland Transit Administration
Sponsoring majorities in both chambers (HB 1081 / SB 947)
opponents: A minority of legislators voted no (House 36 and 32 no votes on the two bills)
Property-rights advocates wary of expanded quick-take authority

sources.label: Ballotpedia News — MD eminent domain amendment certified

issues.title: Maryland Collective Bargaining for State Employees Amendment (Nov 2026)
issues.summary: Legislatively referred constitutional amendment (SB 28 / HB 604) requiring the governor's annual budget to fund the wages, hours, benefits, and employment terms set in collective-bargaining memoranda of understanding covering certain state employees. Companion law establishes binding arbitration by a neutral arbitrator, replacing the current advisory fact-finding that left the governor with discretion.
issues.body:
What The Amendment Does

  • Requires the governor to include in the annual budget the money needed to fund the wages, hours, benefits, and other employment terms agreed to in collective-bargaining memoranda of understanding with covered state employees.
  • Works alongside companion statutory changes that create binding arbitration before a neutral arbitrator when the state and a union reach impasse.
  • Replaces the prior system of advisory fact-finding, under which the governor retained discretion over whether to fund a negotiated agreement.

How It Got On The Ballot

  • Legislatively referred constitutional amendment (60% supermajority of each chamber required).
  • Cross-filed as SB 28 and HB 604; final passage reported on April 10, 2026, largely along party lines. Precise roll-call counts are not independently confirmed here.
  • A simple statewide majority is required to ratify it on the November 3, 2026 ballot.
issues.analysis: This amendment moves a budgeting decision out of the elected governor's hands and locks it into the constitution: whatever a union negotiates, the budget must fund, and impasses go to a binding arbitrator who answers to no voter. A laborer is worth his wages, and Scripture is emphatic that the hired man must be paid what is just and on time. But the same Scripture that protects the worker also warns against putting the public purse on autopilot. A citizen weighing this should ask who bears the cost when an unelected arbitrator's award must be funded regardless of revenue — the taxpayer — and whether removing the governor's discretion strengthens working families or simply entrenches public-sector union leverage at the expense of budget accountability. Stewardship of the commonwealth's money and fair dealing with its servants are both biblical duties; this measure trades flexibility for a guarantee, and voters should weigh that trade with open eyes.
issues.scripture: Thou shalt not oppress an hired servant that is poor and needy... At his day thou shalt give him his hire, neither shall the sun go down upon it.
supporters: Maryland public-employee unions (e.g., AFSCME-affiliated state workers)
opponents: Republican legislators (no votes on largely party-line passage)
Fiscal-accountability advocates wary of binding-arbitration budget mandates

sources.label: Ballotpedia News — MD collective-bargaining amendment to voters

issues.title: Maryland Commission on Judicial Disabilities Vacancies Amendment (Nov 2026)
issues.summary: Legislatively referred constitutional amendment (SB 933, 2026 session) letting the chair of the Commission on Judicial Disabilities appoint former members to temporarily fill vacancies, and letting the governor appoint a substitute or extend a member's term when a member is recused, disqualified, or has reached the end of a term. The 2026 version replaced an earlier 2025 amendment (HB 788).
issues.body:
What The Amendment Does

  • Allows the chair of the Commission on Judicial Disabilities to appoint former Commission members to temporarily fill vacancies so the body can keep functioning.
  • Lets the governor appoint a substitute member, or extend a sitting member's term, when a member is recused, disqualified, or has reached term's end while a matter is pending.
  • The 2026 revision adds a guardrail: a temporary substitute previously rejected by the Senate may not serve.

How It Got On The Ballot

  • Legislatively referred constitutional amendment (60% supermajority of each chamber required).
  • Carried in 2026 as SB 933; it replaced an earlier version (HB 788) that had been referred in the 2025 session. Reported to have passed both chambers; the precise roll call is not independently confirmed here.
  • A simple statewide majority is required to ratify it on the November 3, 2026 ballot.
issues.analysis: This is administrative plumbing for the body that disciplines Maryland's judges, not a culture-war flashpoint — and that is exactly why a citizen should read it plainly rather than reflexively. A commission that polices judicial misconduct only works if it can keep a quorum; allowing former members to step in and barring Senate-rejected substitutes are reasonable continuity fixes. The one thing worth watching is the expansion of the governor's hand in staffing a body meant to hold the judiciary accountable: any time an executive gains influence over the watchmen of another branch, the separation God built into good government is at stake. On its face this measure is modest and defensible; vote it on the merits of keeping judicial accountability functioning, not on party reflex.
issues.scripture: Judges and officers shalt thou make thee in all thy gates... and they shall judge the people with just judgment.
supporters: Maryland General Assembly (reported to have passed both chambers)
opponents: No organized opposition identified

sources.label: Ballotpedia — Maryland Commission on Judicial Disabilities Vacancies Amendment (2026)
Ballotpedia News — MD legislature updates judicial-disabilities amendment

issues.title: Michigan Constitutional Convention Question (Nov 2026)
issues.summary: An automatically referred ballot question asking whether Michigan should convene a convention of elected delegates to undertake a general revision of the 1963 Michigan Constitution. Any revision the convention produces would still have to be ratified by the voters.
issues.body:
What The Question Asks

  • Ballot language: whether a convention of elected delegates should be called for the purpose of a general revision of the Michigan Constitution.
  • If voters approve it, a convention would convene in Lansing (reported date October 5, 2027) and any resulting revision would itself have to be submitted back to the voters for ratification.
  • It is a yes-or-no question about reopening the whole charter, not a vote on any specific change.

How It Got On The Ballot

  • This is an automatic referral, not a citizen initiative or a legislative referral. Article XII, Section 3 of the 1963 Michigan Constitution requires the convention question be put to voters in 1978 and every 16 years after — it last appeared in 2010 and would next appear in 2042 if rejected now.
  • No petition signatures or legislative vote were required to place it on the ballot.
  • Michigan voters rejected the same question in 1978, 1994, and 2010.
issues.analysis: A constitutional convention does not amend one clause — it reopens the entire charter at once, and everything from property rights to the structure of the courts is back on the table. That is a far heavier decision than any single amendment, because the citizen cannot know in advance what a convention of delegates will produce; he can only judge whether the present constitution is broken badly enough to risk a wholesale rewrite. Scripture warns against casually moving the boundary stones the fathers set. The prudent question is not whether the 1963 constitution is perfect, but whether the people trust the delegates and special interests who would dominate a convention to hand back something better, and whether ordinary families would gain or lose protections in the trade. Demand specifics before voting yes on a blank check.
issues.scripture: Remove not the ancient landmark, which thy fathers have set.
supporters: Michigan House Speaker Matt Hall (R-Richland Twp.)
Senate Minority Leader Aric Nesbitt (R-Porter Twp.)
opponents: 'Protect MI Constitution from Special Interests' (registered ballot-question committee opposing the measure)

issues.title: Minnesota Permanent School Fund Amendment (HF 3900, Nov 2026)
issues.summary: A legislatively referred constitutional amendment changing how Minnesota's Permanent School Fund is invested and paid out — replacing the rule that the fund principal stay 'perpetual and inviolate' with a requirement to preserve the fund's purchasing power, and letting school districts receive roughly 4.5% of the fund's three-year average value each year instead of only its interest and dividends.
issues.body:
What The Amendment Does

  • Replaces the constitutional requirement that the Permanent School Fund principal remain 'perpetual and inviolate' with language requiring the fund's purchasing power to be preserved.
  • Allows annual distributions to school districts of about 4.5% of the fund's three-year rolling average value, rather than limiting payouts to interest and dividends earned.
  • Sponsor's stated rationale is to send more money to schools 'at no cost to property tax payers.'

How It Got On The Ballot

  • Legislatively referred constitutional amendment carried in House File 3900; cited sponsor State Sen. Mary Kunesh (DFL-39). Conference-committee final version issued May 16, 2026.
  • Reported votes (May 16, 2026): House 134-0; Senate 43-24.
  • Minnesota has no citizen-initiative process — a simple majority of each chamber refers an amendment to the voters. At ratification it needs a majority of ALL ballots cast in the election, so a blank vote effectively counts as a no.
issues.analysis: The Permanent School Fund is a trust — land granted at statehood, held so that its earnings, not its corpus, support each generation of schoolchildren in turn. The old 'perpetual and inviolate' rule is a fence around the principal; this amendment trades that fence for a 'purchasing power' standard and a fixed annual draw. There is a real stewardship case on both sides. Drawing 4.5% of a three-year average can send schools steadier money and beat the slow erosion of inflation; but it can also spend down a trust faster than it grows in a weak market, quietly shifting wealth from children's children to the present budget. A good man leaves an inheritance to his children's children — so the citizen should ask whether the new draw is genuinely sustainable or just a more comfortable way to dip into the corpus.
issues.scripture: A good man leaveth an inheritance to his children's children: and the wealth of the sinner is laid up for the just.
supporters: State Sen. Mary Kunesh (DFL-39), cited sponsor
The Minnesota House, which passed it 134-0
opponents: Senate Republicans, 24 of whom voted no; no organized opposition campaign was identified in the sources

sources.label: Ballotpedia News — Minnesota voters to decide on school-fund amendment (May 19, 2026)

issues.title: Missouri Show-Me Prosperity Investment Fund Amendment (SJR 95, Nov 2026)
issues.summary: A legislatively referred constitutional amendment creating a state investment fund (a sovereign-wealth-style fund) that would invest in S&P 500 exchange-traded funds, cap annual withdrawals at 3%, and aim over time to build a return stream that could offset or eventually replace state tax revenue.
issues.body:
What The Amendment Does

  • Establishes a state-owned investment fund (described as the 'Show-Me Prosperity' fund) that would invest public dollars in S&P 500 exchange-traded funds.
  • Caps annual withdrawals from the fund at 3% of its value.
  • Stated long-term goal is to grow a return stream large enough to eventually reduce or replace tax revenue the state now collects.

How It Got On The Ballot

  • Legislatively referred constitutional amendment carried in Senate Joint Resolution 95; sponsor Sen. Adam Schnelting (R, St. Charles).
  • Reported votes: Senate 22-7 (March 23, 2026); House 85-56 (May 15, 2026).
  • Ballotpedia News reported the General Assembly placed two constitutional amendments on the November 3, 2026 ballot in this batch (this fund and the county-sheriff amendment).
issues.analysis: A state that puts public money into the market is making a wager with other people's taxes. The instinct behind a sovereign-style fund is sound stewardship — wealth gathered patiently grows, and a 3% withdrawal cap is a real guardrail against a legislature raiding the principal in a lean year. But the same fund exposes the public treasury to market downturns, and 'we will eventually replace your taxes' is the kind of promise that outlives the politicians who make it. The wise man keeps treasure and oil in his dwelling; the fool spends it all. The citizen should weigh whether Missouri has the discipline to leave the fund alone in good years and bad, or whether a market fund simply becomes a new pot for the connected to reach into.
issues.scripture: There is treasure to be desired and oil in the dwelling of the wise; but a foolish man spendeth it up.
supporters: Sen. Adam Schnelting (R, St. Charles), sponsor
The legislative majorities that referred it (Senate 22-7, House 85-56)
opponents: The minority that voted no (7 senators and 56 representatives)

sources.label: Ballotpedia News — Missouri General Assembly puts two amendments on the Nov 3 ballot (May 21, 2026)

issues.title: Missouri Election of County Sheriffs Amendment (SJR 87, Nov 2026)
issues.summary: A legislatively referred constitutional amendment that would write into the Missouri Constitution that county sheriffs are elected to four-year terms and serve as the chief law-enforcement officer of their county, with named exceptions for St. Louis City, St. Louis County, and St. Charles County.
issues.body:
What The Amendment Does

  • Constitutionalizes the elected office of county sheriff, serving a four-year term, as the chief law-enforcement officer of the county.
  • Provides exceptions for St. Louis City, St. Louis County, and St. Charles County, whose arrangements differ.
  • Puts the office's elected status beyond the easy reach of ordinary legislation.

How It Got On The Ballot

  • Legislatively referred constitutional amendment carried in Senate Joint Resolution 87; sponsor Sen. Jill Carter (R-32, Neosho).
  • Reported votes: House 95-46; Senate 24-9 (May 15, 2026).
  • Placed on the November 3, 2026 ballot alongside the state investment-fund amendment per Ballotpedia News.
issues.analysis: The office of sheriff is the oldest elected lawman in the Anglo-American tradition — answerable to the county that votes him in, not to a distant appointing authority. Scripture frames the magistrate as God's minister for the public good, and there is a sound conservative case that a sheriff who must face the voters is more accountable than one installed from above. This amendment locks that elected status into the constitution. The fair questions are narrow: whether enshrining the office this way meaningfully protects local accountability or merely freezes the status quo, and whether the carve-outs for the St. Louis jurisdictions are principled or political. For most of Missouri this is a modest, structurally conservative measure.
issues.scripture: For he is the minister of God to thee for good. But if thou do that which is evil, be afraid; for he beareth not the sword in vain.
supporters: The legislative majorities that referred it (House 95-46, Senate 24-9)
opponents: The minority that voted no (46 representatives and 9 senators)

issues.title: Nebraska Legislative Term Limits Amendment (LR19CA, Nov 2026)
issues.summary: A legislatively referred constitutional amendment that would lengthen term limits for Nebraska state senators from two consecutive four-year terms (eight years) to three consecutive four-year terms (twelve years). The current eight-year limit was approved by Nebraska voters in 2000 and took effect in 2006.
issues.body:
What The Amendment Does

  • Extends the limit on consecutive service in Nebraska's Legislature from two four-year terms (eight years) to three four-year terms (twelve years).
  • A senator who serves the maximum would still face the existing sit-out before becoming eligible again.
  • It loosens, rather than removes, the term limit voters wrote into the constitution in 2000.

How It Got On The Ballot

  • Legislatively referred constitutional amendment carried in Legislative Resolution 19CA; primary sponsor State Sen. Rob Dover (District 19). Nebraska's Legislature is unicameral and officially nonpartisan.
  • Reported vote: the Legislature adopted the referral 39-10 on May 28, 2025 (a referral requires a 60 percent supermajority, or 30 of 49 votes).
  • Placed on the November 3, 2026 ballot; voters last set the eight-year limit in 2000.
issues.analysis: Term limits were the people's own check on entrenched power, written into the constitution by direct vote in 2000 precisely so that no lawmaker could settle in for a career. This amendment does not abolish that check, but it does relax it by half a term. The honest case for the change is that eight years is barely enough time to learn a complex job and that experience is handed instead to lobbyists and permanent staff. The honest case against is that the longer a man holds a seat, the safer the incumbency and the harder the unseating, and that the voters already weighed this and chose eight years. Either way, the deciding question belongs to the citizen who set the limit in the first place: does Nebraska gain more in seasoned legislators than it risks in entrenched ones. Weigh it on accountability, not on any one senator.
issues.scripture: When the righteous are in authority, the people rejoice: but when the wicked beareth rule, the people mourn.
supporters: The legislative majority that referred it (39-10)
opponents: The ten senators who voted against the referral

issues.title: New Hampshire Abolish Office of Register of Probate Amendment (Question 1, Nov 2026)
issues.summary: A legislatively referred constitutional amendment, appearing as Question 1, that would strike all references to the office of register of probate from the New Hampshire Constitution. The office was left largely vestigial after the state's 2011 court reorganization moved most of its duties elsewhere. Ratification requires a two-thirds vote of the electorate.
issues.body:
What The Amendment Does

  • Removes every reference to the office of register of probate from the New Hampshire Constitution.
  • The office's substantive duties were already transferred in the 2011 court reorganization, leaving the constitutional references obsolete.
  • It is administrative housekeeping, not a change to any policy, court power, or citizen right.

How It Got On The Ballot

  • Legislatively referred constitutional amendment carried in CACR 13 (2026 session); sponsor Rep. Dennis Mannion (R). New Hampshire has no citizen-initiative process — amendments require a three-fifths vote of each chamber to refer, then two-thirds of voters to ratify.
  • Reported votes: House 325-15 (Feb 5, 2026); Senate 23-1 (Mar 26, 2026) — both well above the three-fifths referral threshold.
  • A near-identical measure in 2022 won 62.9 percent voter support but fell short of the two-thirds needed to ratify.
issues.analysis: This is about as low-stakes as a constitutional amendment gets. The office it deletes has been a dead letter since 2011, and the change touches no power, tax, or liberty — it merely cleans obsolete language out of the state charter. There is a quiet virtue in keeping the founding document honest and uncluttered, so that what it says matches what actually governs. The only real friction is the two-thirds bar: a sensible cleanup like this failed in 2022 not because voters opposed it but because too few bothered to vote yes. For the citizen, the question is simply whether to tidy a vestige the legislature has already retired by an overwhelming, bipartisan margin.
issues.scripture: Let all things be done decently and in order.
supporters: The bipartisan legislative supermajorities that referred it (House 325-15, Senate 23-1)
opponents: The handful of legislators who voted no (15 representatives and 1 senator)

sources.label: Ballotpedia News — New Hampshire voters to decide amendment to eliminate register of probate references (Mar 31, 2026)

issues.title: New Mexico Eliminate Governor's Pocket Veto and Require Veto Explanations Amendment (Nov 2026)
issues.summary: A legislatively referred constitutional amendment that would end the governor's “pocket veto.” A bill the governor neither signs nor vetoes within the constitutional window would become law automatically, and any veto would have to carry a substantive written explanation to the legislature.
issues.body:
What The Amendment Does

  • Ends the pocket veto: a bill the governor neither signs nor returns within the constitutional window becomes law instead of dying.
  • Requires the governor to give the legislature a substantive written reason for any veto.
  • Would make New Mexico the 38th state without a pocket veto.

How It Got On The Ballot

  • Legislatively referred constitutional amendment carried in HJR 2 (2025 session). New Mexico has no citizen-initiative process; constitutional amendments reach the ballot only by legislative referral.
  • Reported votes: House 66-0 (Feb 27, 2025); Senate 38-0 (Mar 13, 2025) — unanimous in both chambers.
  • Source note: one advocacy tracker labels the resolution “SJR 4”; Ballotpedia reports it as HJR 2. The substance and unanimous votes are consistent across sources.
issues.analysis: This is a check-and-balance question more than a partisan one — the unanimous votes show it. A pocket veto lets an executive kill a bill by simply sitting on it, with no public reason given; ending it forces the decision into the open and makes the governor own a veto in writing. For a citizen who values government that answers for what it does, that transparency cuts in a healthy direction. The practical stakes are modest — it changes process, not policy — but keeping the lines of accountability clean is itself a public good.
issues.scripture: For there is nothing hid, which shall not be manifested; neither was any thing kept secret, but that it should come abroad.
supporters: The bipartisan legislative supermajorities that referred it (House 66-0, Senate 38-0)
opponents: No organized opposition recorded; the measure passed both chambers unanimously

issues.title: New Mexico Board of Regents Nominating Committees Amendment (Nov 2026)
issues.summary: A legislatively referred constitutional amendment that keeps the governor's power to appoint university Boards of Regents but requires non-student nominees to be drawn from lists vetted by bipartisan nominating committees.
issues.body:
What The Amendment Does

  • Keeps the governor's authority to appoint members of university Boards of Regents.
  • Requires non-student regent appointees to be chosen from lists screened by bipartisan nominating committees (which include student representatives).
  • Adds a vetting layer intended to professionalize appointments to university governing boards.

How It Got On The Ballot

  • Legislatively referred constitutional amendment carried in HJR 1 (2026 session); sponsor Rep. Christine Chandler.
  • Reported votes: House 67-0; Senate 34-7.
issues.analysis: This is inside-baseball governance — who screens the people who run state universities. The case for it is that bipartisan vetting makes regent seats less of a pure political reward and more of a competence question; the case against is that it adds a committee between voters' elected governor and the appointment, diffusing accountability. Neither cuts deeply on the issues this scorecard tracks. For most citizens this is a low-stakes structural tweak; weigh it on whether you trust nominating committees more or less than a single elected governor.
issues.scripture: Moreover thou shalt provide out of all the people able men, such as fear God, men of truth, hating covetousness; and place such over them.
supporters: The supermajorities that referred it (House 67-0, Senate 34-7)
opponents: The seven senators who voted no

issues.title: New Mexico Establish Salary for State Legislators Amendment (Nov 2026)
issues.summary: A legislatively referred constitutional amendment that would, for the first time, pay New Mexico legislators a salary tied to the state's median household income, set by an independent citizens commission. New Mexico is currently the only state with an unpaid legislature.
issues.body:
What The Amendment Does

  • Establishes a salary for state legislators, who currently serve unpaid (New Mexico is the only state with an unpaid legislature).
  • Ties the pay level to New Mexico's median household income (about $67,800).
  • Creates a nine-member independent Citizens Commission on Legislative Salaries to set the amount, rather than letting legislators set their own pay.

How It Got On The Ballot

  • Legislatively referred constitutional amendment carried in HJR 5 (2026 session).
  • Reported votes: House 41-26; Senate 23-19 — referred over notable dissent in both chambers.
issues.analysis: There's an honest argument on both sides here. Paying legislators can open the citizen-legislature to people who aren't independently wealthy or retired — a fairness point. The counterargument is that an unpaid, part-time legislature stays closer to ordinary life and resists becoming a career class. The amendment's guardrail is real: an independent commission sets the pay, not the legislators themselves, and it is pegged to what a typical household earns rather than a number lawmakers pick. The split votes show this is no slam dunk. Decide it on whether you think modest pay broadens the bench or professionalizes a body better kept amateur.
issues.scripture: The labourer is worthy of his hire.
supporters: The legislative majorities that referred it (House 41-26, Senate 23-19)
opponents: The 26 representatives and 19 senators who voted no — a substantial minority wary of paying a historically volunteer legislature

Ballotpedia — New Mexico Legislative Salary Amendment (2026)

issues.title: New Mexico Allow School Elections on Partisan Election Dates Amendment (Nov 2026)
issues.summary: A legislatively referred constitutional amendment that would repeal the requirement that school-board and school elections be held separately, allowing them to be consolidated with regular state and federal partisan elections.
issues.body:
What The Amendment Does

  • Repeals the constitutional rule that school-board and school elections be held on their own separate dates.
  • Lets those elections be consolidated onto regular state or federal partisan election dates.
  • Aim is higher turnout and lower cost by folding off-cycle school elections into the general election.

How It Got On The Ballot

  • Legislatively referred constitutional amendment carried in SJR 1 (2026 session).
  • Reported votes: Senate 38-0; House 50-9.
issues.analysis: Off-cycle school elections are notorious for tiny turnout — a few percent of voters decide who runs the schools and how the bond money is spent. Folding them into the November general would put those choices in front of far more citizens, which generally favors broader accountability. The tradeoff is that school races would now share a ballot with partisan contests, and critics worry that nationalizes what were nonpartisan local school questions. On balance this is a turnout question with election-integrity overtones; if you think more eyes on school governance is healthy, it points yes.
issues.scripture: Where no counsel is, the people fall: but in the multitude of counsellors there is safety.
supporters: The legislators who referred it (Senate 38-0, House 50-9)
opponents: The nine representatives who voted no
Those who prefer school elections kept separate from partisan contests

issues.title: New Mexico Higher Education Improvements Bond (Nov 2026)
issues.summary: A general-obligation bond question authorizing about $230 million for capital improvements at higher-education institutions, special schools, and tribal schools, repaid through a statewide property tax.
issues.body:
What The Bond Does

  • Authorizes roughly $230 million in general-obligation bonds for capital improvements at higher-education institutions, special schools, and tribal schools.
  • General-obligation bonds are repaid over time through a statewide property tax.
  • One of three bond questions referred together by HB 248 (about $280 million total).

How It Got On The Ballot

  • Referred by HB 248 (2026 session); House 65-0, Senate 38-0; signed March 10, 2026.
issues.analysis: Bond questions are a stewardship call: real needs versus real debt. The money funds bricks-and-mortar improvements at colleges and schools, and the unanimous legislative votes signal broad agreement the projects are warranted. The honest cost is that general-obligation bonds are repaid with interest through property taxes, so a yes is a yes to long-term borrowing. The discipline a careful citizen applies is to ask whether each project is a genuine capital need or a wish-list item, and whether the state's existing debt load leaves room. The dollar figures here are public; weigh them against your tolerance for property-tax-backed debt.
issues.scripture: For which of you, intending to build a tower, sitteth not down first, and counteth the cost, whether he have sufficient to finish it?
supporters: The legislature, which referred it unanimously (House 65-0, Senate 38-0)
Higher-education and tribal-school institutions slated for the funds
opponents: Fiscal conservatives wary of new property-tax-backed general-obligation debt

issues.title: New Mexico Senior Citizen Facilities Bond (Nov 2026)
issues.summary: A general-obligation bond question authorizing about $30 million for the construction, improvement, and equipping of senior-citizen facilities, repaid through a statewide property tax.
issues.body:
What The Bond Does

  • Authorizes roughly $30 million in general-obligation bonds to construct, improve, and equip senior-citizen facilities.
  • Repaid over time through a statewide property tax.
  • One of three bond questions referred together by HB 248 (about $280 million total).

How It Got On The Ballot

  • Referred by HB 248 (2026 session); House 65-0, Senate 38-0; signed March 10, 2026.
issues.analysis: Of the three bond questions, this is the smallest and arguably the most concrete — facilities that serve elderly citizens. Honoring the older generation is a plain biblical duty, and $30 million is a modest figure against a $280 million package. The same stewardship caution applies: it is borrowed money repaid with interest through property taxes. But the purpose is tangible and the sum restrained. A citizen weighing it asks whether the facilities are genuinely needed and well-run, not whether caring for seniors is worthwhile — that part isn't in question.
issues.scripture: Thou shalt rise up before the hoary head, and honour the face of the old man, and fear thy God: I am the LORD.

issues.title: New Mexico Library Acquisition Bond (Nov 2026)
issues.summary: A general-obligation bond question authorizing about $20 million for acquisitions at public, school, academic, and tribal libraries, repaid through a statewide property tax.
issues.body:
What The Bond Does

  • Authorizes roughly $20 million in general-obligation bonds for acquisitions at public, school, academic, and tribal libraries.
  • Repaid over time through a statewide property tax.
  • Smallest of three bond questions referred together by HB 248 (about $280 million total).

How It Got On The Ballot

  • Referred by HB 248 (2026 session); House 65-0, Senate 38-0; signed March 10, 2026.
issues.analysis: The smallest of the three bonds, $20 million for library materials across the state. The stewardship questions are identical to its companion measures: a real but borrowed sum, repaid through property taxes. Libraries are a long-standing public good, and the figure is modest. As with the other bonds, the discipline is to weigh the concrete benefit against the debt, and to remember that approving all three is approving the full $280 million package. Decide each on its merits and on your tolerance for property-tax-backed borrowing.

issues.title: North Carolina Lower Maximum Income Tax Rate Amendment (Nov 2026)
issues.summary: A legislatively referred constitutional amendment that would lower North Carolina's constitutional cap on the state income tax rate from 7 percent to 3.5 percent — just below the current flat rate of 3.99 percent.
issues.body:
What The Amendment Does

  • Lowers the constitutional ceiling on the state personal income tax rate from 7 percent to 3.5 percent.
  • The current flat income tax rate is 3.99 percent, so the new cap would sit just below today's rate, tightly constraining future increases.
  • Bars the legislature from raising the income tax above 3.5 percent without amending the constitution again.

How It Got On The Ballot

  • Referred via Senate Bill 1080 (2026). North Carolina has no citizen initiative; amendments require a three-fifths vote of each chamber.
  • Reported votes: Senate 30-18; House 73-46.
issues.analysis: This is a tax-limitation question with real teeth. Locking a 3.5 percent ceiling into the constitution makes future income-tax hikes much harder — a durable restraint that favors taxpayers and predictable budgeting. The flip side is the mirror image of the same fact: a constitutional cap ties the hands of future legislatures and voters, including in a downturn. For citizens who prize limited government and guarding the household against tax creep, this points clearly yes. Those who want fiscal flexibility kept in elected hands will weigh it differently. The largely party-line votes reflect that divide.

issues.title: North Carolina Property Tax Levy Limit Amendment (Nov 2026)
issues.summary: A legislatively referred constitutional amendment that would require the General Assembly to enact laws limiting local property-tax increases and bar local governments from levying property taxes except for purposes set by uniform general law, unless approved by voters.
issues.body:
What The Amendment Does

  • Directs the General Assembly to enact laws limiting how much local governments can raise property taxes.
  • Bars local governments from levying property taxes except for purposes authorized by uniform general law — unless a majority of qualified voters approve.
  • Shifts some control over local property taxation toward the state and toward direct voter approval.

How It Got On The Ballot

  • Referred via House Bill 1089 (2026). Amendments require a three-fifths vote of each chamber.
  • Reported votes: Senate 31-15; House 73-46.
issues.analysis: This is a property-tax restraint aimed at local governments — a guard on one of the taxes families feel most directly through their homes. By requiring voter approval for taxes outside what general law authorizes, it pushes a check toward citizens. The tension is between local control and statewide limits: critics argue it hamstrings counties and towns that know their own needs, while supporters see a needed brake on local tax growth. For a citizen who wants the household protected from rising property-tax bills and more say over local levies, it leans yes. Those who prioritize local self-government will weigh the loss of municipal flexibility.
issues.scripture: Remove not the ancient landmark, which thy fathers have set.

opponents: The 15 senators and 46 representatives who voted no

issues.title: North Dakota 60% Vote Requirement for Constitutional Amendments Measure (Nov 2026)
issues.summary: A legislatively referred constitutional amendment that would require any future constitutional amendment — whether citizen-initiated or legislature-referred — to receive a 60 percent supermajority of votes to pass, replacing the current simple-majority threshold.
issues.body:
What The Measure Does

  • Raises the threshold to adopt any constitutional amendment from a simple majority (50%+1) to a 60 percent supermajority.
  • Applies to both citizen-initiated and legislature-referred amendments.
  • Makes North Dakota's constitution harder to change at the ballot box.

How It Got On The Ballot

  • Referred via House Concurrent Resolution 3003 (2025 session).
  • Reported votes: House 65-28 (Jan 29, 2025); Senate 33-13 (Apr 9, 2025).
issues.analysis: This is a question about how easily the state's foundational law can be rewritten. Supporters argue a 60 percent bar protects the constitution from being amended by narrow, well-funded campaigns and keeps it stable; critics counter that it dilutes majority rule and makes citizen initiatives — a check on the legislature — much harder to win. There is a genuine conservative case on both sides: reverence for a stable charter versus the people's direct power to amend it. Note the self-interested angle — the legislature is asking voters to make the initiative process, which can bypass the legislature, harder to use. Weigh whether you value constitutional stability or an accessible citizen check more.

issues.title: North Dakota State Legislative Term Limits Amendment (Nov 2026)
issues.summary: A legislatively referred constitutional amendment that would rewrite the term limits North Dakota voters added in 2022 — letting legislators serve up to four four-year terms in either chamber, excluding partial terms, and repealing the 2022 clause that barred the legislature from amending term limits.
issues.body:
What The Amendment Does

  • Rewrites the legislative term limits voters approved as 2022 Measure 1.
  • Would let legislators serve up to four four-year terms (effectively 16 years) in either chamber, and would not count partial terms toward the limit.
  • Repeals the 2022 clause that barred the legislature from amending term limits.

How It Got On The Ballot

  • Referred via Senate Concurrent Resolution 4008 (2025 session); Senate 24-23, House 53-39 (on reconsideration).
  • A 2026 lawsuit challenged whether the legislature could refer changes to a citizen-enacted measure; the North Dakota Supreme Court allowed the measure to remain on the November ballot.
issues.analysis: This is the legislature asking voters to loosen term limits that those same voters imposed just four years ago — and to repeal the very clause meant to stop the legislature from doing this. That history is the heart of the question. Supporters say experienced legislators serve constituents better and that 16 years is still a real limit; critics see incumbents trying to extend their own careers and undo a citizen vote. The one-vote Senate margin and the lawsuit show how contested it is. For a citizen who values the 2022 vote and is wary of legislators rewriting their own limits, the skeptical read is warranted; weigh it against any case for retained experience.

issues.title: Ohio Voter Photo ID Constitutional Amendment (Nov 2026)
issues.summary: A legislatively referred constitutional amendment that would write Ohio's existing photo-ID voting requirement into the state constitution, requiring in-person voters to present government-issued photo ID and setting identity-verification rules for absentee voters.
issues.body:
What The Amendment Does

  • Enshrines Ohio's existing photo-ID voting requirement in the state constitution.
  • Requires in-person voters to present government-issued photo ID.
  • For absentee voters, permits either a photo ID or a signature plus another authorized unique identifier; allows the legislature to create an alternative verification process for voters with sincere religious objections to being photographed.

How It Got On The Ballot

  • Legislatively referred constitutional amendment carried in Senate Joint Resolution 10 (SJR 10); sponsors Sens. Jane Timken and Theresa Gavarone.
  • Reported votes: Senate 22-9 (Jun 3, 2026); House 61-27 (Jun 10, 2026) — both clearing the 60 percent referral threshold. Certified on House passage.
issues.analysis: Ohio already requires photo ID by statute; this would lock it into the constitution so a future legislature or court couldn't easily undo it. For citizens who see voter ID as a basic, common-sense safeguard of election integrity, putting it beyond the reach of ordinary politics is the appeal. Opponents argue ID rules can burden eligible voters and that constitutionalizing them is rigid; the religious-objection carve-out is meant to soften that edge. The votes split largely along party lines. If election integrity ranks high for you and you trust the existing ID framework, this points yes; the live questions are constitutional rigidity and access, not the ID requirement itself, which is already law.
